As a deepening area of low pressure lifted northeastward into the Great Lakes, strong northwesterly winds gusting over 40mph brought sharply colder air into central Illinois during the night of January 12th.  Temperatures plunged into the single digits above zero by the morning of January 13th, then didn't climb back into the teens until the afternoon of January 17th.  The coldest day during this period was January 14th when high temperatures remained below zero and overnight lows plummeted into the teens below zero everywhere north of the I-70 corridor.  Record cold high temperatures were established at many sites: including -5 at Peoria, -3 at Springfield, and -3 at Lincoln.  Winds were strongest immediately behind the departing system into January 13th, but remained a factor until air temperatures gradually warmed.  The resulting wind-chill values dropped as low as -25 to -35 at times, creating extremely dangerous conditions for anyone venturing outdoors.  An 85 year-old woman from Pekin in Tazewell County died on January 16th as a result of hypothermia.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brisk northwesterly winds in conjunction with air temperatures dipping below zero resulted in dangerously cold wind-chill values bottoming out at 25 below to 35 below zero at times.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Bitterly cold air combined with winds gusting to 35 mph at times allowed wind chills to drop to 30 below to 45 below zero across all of northern Illinois from the early morning of January 14th through the morning of January 15th.  The coldest wind chills of around 45 below zero occurred around and just after daybreak on January 14th.  Air temperatures ranged from 10 below to 15 below zero on the morning of January 14th and from 15 below to 20 below on the morning of January 15th.  Wind chills between 20 below and 30 below continued for the rest of January 15th and through the morning of January 17th when temperatures slowly warmed into the single digits above zero and winds began to diminish.||A 41 year old woman was found dead outside a library in Coal City from cold exposure on January 14th and a 59 year old man was found dead outside his home in Bolingbrook from cold exposure on January 16th.

A rapidly deepening low pressure system, with one of the lowest pressure values to occur in January in this region, tracked from near St. Louis towards Chicago during the day on the 12th. Heavy rain and thunderstorms moved through Southern Illinois during the morning, with the stronger wind gusts following the heavy rain, primarily out of the west during the afternoon to evening hours. The strongest gust recorded in S. IL was 44 mph at the Cairo AWOS.

Precipitation streamed east-northeastward across the Tennessee River Valley on the 14th and 15th. The northern fringes of this system crossed the Quad State in two primary waves, one on the 14th during the afternoon into the early evening, and the second on the 15th during the morning. The first wave on the 14th resulted in 1-2'' of snowfall in far southern Illinois while, further north, snowfall amounts were less than an inch. The second wave on the 15th was limited to flurries in Southern Illinois.

As a deep area of low pressure tracked slowly northeastward across the Upper Midwest, a warm front surged northward through central Illinois on February 8th.  Thanks to strong southerly flow in the wake of the front, near record high temperatures in the lower to middle 60s were observed.  With modest instability in place and increasing deep-layer wind shear ahead of an approaching upper wave, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Illinois River Valley during the late afternoon and evening.  One of the cells produced an EF-1 tornado in northern Marshall County near Henry that tracked northeastward into Putnam County.  Other storms dropped hail as large as quarters.

An unseasonably warm and humid airmass characterized by high temperatures in the upper 50s and lower 60s during the afternoon of February 21st preceded the approach of a cold front.  As the front sagged into central Illinois and a nocturnal low-level jet interacted with the boundary, scattered thunderstorms developed across north-central Illinois during the pre-dawn hours of February 22nd.  The strongest and longest lasting cell tracked from southern Knox County eastward into northern McLean County, producing hail as large as half dollars at times.

Scattered multicellular convection was expected ahead of a cold front on the night of February 10th. Late in the evening, disorganized cells developed into a loosely organized line of storms that briefly produced severe hail just after crossing the Mississippi River into Randolph County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A Facebook user posted a photo of a hail stone laying next to a ruler. The stone's long edge measured 1 inch.

Record-breaking warmth occurred across central Illinois on February 27th as afternoon high temperatures soared well into the 70s to around 80 degrees.  Despite moderate instability and wind shear, the atmosphere remained largely capped throughout the day before the cap weakened by late evening.  Scattered thunderstorms were then able to develop ahead of an advancing cold front along the I-70 corridor.  A few of the storms produced gusty winds and hail...with one cell dropping quarter-sized hail just east of Sumner in Lawrence County and producing a 50mph wind gust at the Lawrenceville Airport during the pre-dawn hours of February 28th.

Despite mostly mild conditions this month, a cold front moved through and brought a brief period of colder weather and snow to the region. Snow began on the morning of the 16th and became heavy through the early afternoon hours. Snowfall rates exceeded 1 inch per hour, helping to overcome warm ground temperatures and lead to hazardous driving conditions. There were some reports of thundersnow as well, an indication of rare atmospheric instability and particularly intense snowfall. A total of 4 to 6 inches of snow fell in a band near Interstate 70. Numerous traffic accidents were reported during the heart of the storm.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief period of heavy snow brought 4 to 6 inches of snowfall. Snowfall rates exceeded 1 inch per hour and led to hazardous driving conditions. Numerous vehicle accidents were reported including on area interstate highways.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Well above normal rainfall along the Ohio River Valley in January led to rising rivers, with flood stage exceeded slightly at Shawneetown late January, continuing through the first week of February as drier weather allowed floodwaters to recede. Minor flooding resulted in very limited impacts in bottomland areas.

A wintry mix of snow, sleet, and rain overspread the area during the afternoon hours on the 16th as a clipper system moved by. Brief thunder was observed before transitioning to all snow as temperatures fell near to slightly below freezing. Snow continued into the evening hours, before quickly ending. A localized intense band of heavy snow caused reduced visibility around a quarter of a mile along Interstate 64. Snowfall rates of 1 to 2 inches per hour occurred across portions of Jefferson, Wayne, Edwards, and Wabash counties where snow accumulations of 4 to 7 inches where reported. Outside the snow band, a sharp cut off to the south only resulted in 1 to 3 inches of snow accumulation. Snow rates quickly overcame warm pavement temperatures resulting in slick conditions. This led to reports of vehicles off roadways near Du Quoin and multiple slide offs and a stalled semi along Route 130. Traffic eventually came to a complete spot due to multiple accidents. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

A strong cold front moved through S Illinois around 0000-0300CST on Feb 28th as strengthening low pressure moved towards the Great Lakes. Gusty gradient winds occurred ahead of the front during the afternoon to evening hours and behind the front towards early morning. Thunderstorm activity in S Illinois overnight was most organized with the frontal passage. The strongest measured gust was 53 mph at Carbondale ASOS during the early evening on the 27th.

As a deep area of low pressure tracked slowly northeastward across the Upper Midwest, a warm front surged northward through central Illinois on February 8th.  Thanks to strong southerly flow in the wake of the front, near record high temperatures in the lower to middle 60s were observed.  With modest instability in place and increasing deep-layer wind shear ahead of an approaching upper wave, scattered supercell thunderstorms developed across the Illinois River Valley during the late afternoon and evening.  One of the cells produced an EF-1 tornado in northern Marshall County near Henry that tracked northeastward into Putnam County.  Other storms dropped hail as large as quarters.

Surface low pressure tracked along a frontal boundary that advanced into the I-64 corridor of Southern Illinois during the pre-dawn hours on the 10th. These features and warm air advection allowed for development of a line of thunderstorms moving across Southern Illinois from midnight to 0230CST, continuing into Southwest Indiana. MUCAPE was fairly modest at 500 J/kg but effective shear was up to around 40 kts. Hail up to an inch in diameter and wind gusts to 50 mph accompanied some of these storms, the most notable of which went through Franklin County which resulted in reports of hail at Christopher, Buckner, and Benton.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated at  dime to quarter size fell at Campbell Hill as a storm moved eastward across northern Jackson County.

A cold frontal passage made its way through the Quad State region during the afternoon on the 22nd. MUCAPE was limited to around 500 J/kg with 0-3 km helicity at 100-200 m2/s2 in SE Missouri and adjacent portions of S Illinois, with less favorable conditions further east. The frontal boundary became a focus for a line of thunderstorms, with mid-level lapse rates and shear robust enough to produce hail, with marginally severe hail reported out of Anna, IL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Media relayed a report of nickel size hail covering the ground in Anna, with a delayed report of 1 inch hail.

Supercell thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary from central Missouri into south-central Illinois along the I-70 corridor during the late afternoon and evening of May 26th.  The storms eventually congealed into a line and swept eastward, causing significant wind damage across the southern portions of Clay, Richland, and Lawrence counties.  Damage surveys revealed two tornadoes touched down...an EF-2 in Clay County northwest of Xenia and an EF-1 in Lawrence County south of Sumner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree and a power line were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along East 250th Avenue just north of North 700th Street at 8:27 PM CDT. The tornado then caused minor damage to a home along North 850th Street before lifting just north of the intersection of North 850th Street and East 900th Avenue at 8:37 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy shallow dense fog formed over much of Southern Illinois during the early morning hours of the 18th. Surface winds were weak with high pressure moving in, while temperatures cooled to the crossover temperature. Surface moisture was plentiful with recent rainfall. Greater coverage/duration of fog occurred near rivers, such as at Carbondale ASOS.

A weak surface trough triggered scattered strong thunderstorms across central Illinois during the late afternoon and evening of May 27th.  One of the cells produced wind gusts of around 60mph and caused minor damage in Pekin in Tazewell County, while another storm dropped penny-sized hail in Decatur in Macon County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Above normal April rainfall in much of Missouri, Illinois, and Southeast Iowa led to elevated river levels early May on the Mississippi River. River levels at Cape Girardeau and Thebes reached minor flood early in the month, albeit for a short duration at Thebes. Rainy weather May 5th-8th and 14th-17th extended minor flood conditions at Cape Girardeau through the first three weeks of May.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Brief minor flooding occurred along the Mississippi River at Thebes early in the month.

There were several rounds of severe storms on May 7th, 2024. An early morning line of storms brought wind damage to a large portion of Iowa, Illinois, and northeast Missouri.  In the early afternoon, a few storms brought large hail to northwest Illinois, followed by another round of gusty storms during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management relayed a report of lots of pea to dime size hail, with a few quarters.

Heavy rainfall in early May, culminating in the May 8th severe weather and flooding event, resulted in rising river levels on the Lower Ohio, Big Muddy, and Mississippi Rivers, with Thebes returning to minor flood and Murphysboro, Olmsted, and Cairo reaching flood stage shortly after. Minor flooding in Southern Illinois around these river sites lasted 5 to 8 days with impacts limited to low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Ohio River near Olmsted in low-lying areas.

Scattered thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the late morning and afternoon of May 24th.||High winds developed across parts of east central Illinois during the afternoon and continued into the early evening.  A private weather station gusted to 61 mph near Claytonville in Iroquois County.  In Ford County, A large evergreen tree was uprooted onto a garage and power lines near Cherry Street and Pells Street in Paxton; A large tree limb was blown down onto a car near Orleans Avenue and Washington Street in Paxton and utility poles were blown down near the intersection of County Roads 1100N and 1300E near Melvin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Dime to quarter size hail was reported along Laraway Road near Nelson Road and Cedar Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across northern Illinois during the late morning and afternoon of May 24th.||High winds developed across parts of east central Illinois during the afternoon and continued into the early evening.  A private weather station gusted to 61 mph near Claytonville in Iroquois County.  In Ford County, A large evergreen tree was uprooted onto a garage and power lines near Cherry Street and Pells Street in Paxton; A large tree limb was blown down onto a car near Orleans Avenue and Washington Street in Paxton and utility poles were blown down near the intersection of County Roads 1100N and 1300E near Melvin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter size hail was reported near University Parkway and Monee Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm produced a weak tornado in east of Macomb Illinois, before a more widespread line of storms moved east through Iowa into Illinois in the overnight hours. The line of storms brought reports of large hail and damaging wind to portions of the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Pictures and video from social media indicated a brief landspout tornado in open fields. No damage has been reported. Times and location estimated.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Patchy shallow dense fog formed over much of Southern Illinois during the early morning hours of the 18th. Surface winds were weak with high pressure moving in, while temperatures cooled to the crossover temperature. Surface moisture was plentiful with recent rainfall. Greater coverage/duration of fog occurred near rivers, such as at Carbondale ASOS.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm front provided focus for convective initiation during the afternoon and evening of March 13th. Discrete thunderstorms grew upscale and produced up to 1.75 inch hail in portions of southwest Illinois with the largest stones falling in southern Monroe and Randolph Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An emergency manager reported power lines down on the south side of Red Bud, IL. No further information was given on the power lines. This was the same thunderstorm that produced golf ball sized hail in Ruma to the south.

Supercell thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary from central Missouri into south-central Illinois along the I-70 corridor during the late afternoon and evening of May 26th.  The storms eventually congealed into a line and swept eastward, causing significant wind damage across the southern portions of Clay, Richland, and Lawrence counties.  Damage surveys revealed two tornadoes touched down...an EF-2 in Clay County northwest of Xenia and an EF-1 in Lawrence County south of Sumner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was snapped and power lines were knocked down.

Heavy rainfall in early May, culminating in the May 8th severe weather and flooding event, resulted in rising river levels on the Lower Ohio, Big Muddy, and Mississippi Rivers, with Thebes returning to minor flood and Murphysboro, Olmsted, and Cairo reaching flood stage shortly after. Minor flooding in Southern Illinois around these river sites lasted 5 to 8 days with impacts limited to low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Big Muddy River near Murphysboro in low-lying areas.

Supercell thunderstorms developed along an outflow boundary from central Missouri into south-central Illinois along the I-70 corridor during the late afternoon and evening of May 26th.  The storms eventually congealed into a line and swept eastward, causing significant wind damage across the southern portions of Clay, Richland, and Lawrence counties.  Damage surveys revealed two tornadoes touched down...an EF-2 in Clay County northwest of Xenia and an EF-1 in Lawrence County south of Sumner.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado originally touched down in Marion County in the National Weather Service Saint Louis County Warning Area (CWA), then crossed into southwestern Clay County just north of the intersection of Beard Road and Songer Township Lane at 7:35 PM CDT.  It knocked down several trees and power lines before tracking northeastward and doing its greatest damage at a farm on the north end of Penguin Drive where several large metal buildings were demolished.  The tornado then lifted just east of Xenia-Iola Road between Songer Township Lane and Hickory Hill Lane at 7:43 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of May 24th a low pressure system was located over the eastern Dakotas, with Illinois positioned in the warm sector. Over the course of the day, several rounds of thunderstorms occurred. First, a squall line tracked across Iowa and reached Illinois by the morning. The most intense part of this squall line tracked north of central Illinois, but the system did produce one instance of wind damage in Stark County. There was a lull in severe weather during the mid-to-late morning, but around midday the lingering storms began to strengthen, while new clusters of storms developed along the associated outflow boundary. These storms continued throughout the afternoon. A cold front moved into the area during the evening, resulting in additional scattered storm development, however, all of the storms weakened into the late evening as daytime heating was lost. Collectively, the thunderstorms on May 24th produced scattered wind damage, hail, and flash flooding across central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Streets and roads were flooded resulting in road closures. This report was from mPING.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep-layer mixing within a dry pre-frontal airmass resulted in strong southerly winds gusting 40-50mph across central Illinois on May 21st.  Scattered showers and thunderstorms well ahead of the front brought higher momentum air down to the surface causing localized gusts of 55-65 mph during the evening.   Due to recently plowed farm fields and dry conditions, the strong winds were able to loft dust from the fields, creating widespread reductions in visibility...with a few spots reporting near zero visibility at times.  Several major highways were temporarily closed due to the dust...including I-55 near Shirley and McLean in McLean County, I-155 near Hopedale in Tazewell County, and I-74 between LeRoy and Farmer City in McLean and DeWitt counties.  A number of traffic accidents occurred due to the poor visibility, including one that injured 6 people on IL-54 between Mount Pulaski and Chestnut in southern Logan County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Blowing dust reduced visibilities to near zero at times, prompting the temporary closure of I-155 near Hopedale.  No injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the afternoon of May 21st: however, these cells dissipated as they crossed the Mississippi River into a much drier environment across central Illinois.  Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed further east during the evening and were able to transport higher momentum air aloft down to the surface.  Gradient wind gusts were generally 40-50mph, but sporadic gusts as high as 55-65mph were reported in and near the developing showers and storms.  As a result, scattered wind damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 58mph wind gust was measured at AWOS station KTIP.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with a cold front triggered a line of strong to severe thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the pre-dawn hours of May 7th.  The squall line advanced eastward across central Illinois between 5am CDT and 9am CDT in a weakening state: however, scattered wind damage was observed.  One of the hardest hit locations was Effingham, where downburst winds of 70mph created minor structural damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Siding, roof, and mailbox damage occurred to a home in the 200 block of West Jaycee Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the afternoon of May 21st: however, these cells dissipated as they crossed the Mississippi River into a much drier environment across central Illinois.  Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed further east during the evening and were able to transport higher momentum air aloft down to the surface.  Gradient wind gusts were generally 40-50mph, but sporadic gusts as high as 55-65mph were reported in and near the developing showers and storms.  As a result, scattered wind damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 60mph wind gust was measured at ASOS station KCMI.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and evening hours of May 27th, a few severe storms moved through Iowa, Illinois, and northeast Missouri. While there were not many storms, they remained strong to severe for most of the entire path through the area, producing large hail reports for the most part, along with some damaging winds, and one isolated EF0 tornado. Most notably, these storms produced significant swaths of small hail, accumulating deep enough to affect roads and leave piles near homes that lasted hours. Some areas of crops were damaged by the small hail and large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8 inch diameter tree limb down. The time was estimated from radar data.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with a cold front triggered a line of strong to severe thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the pre-dawn hours of May 7th.  The squall line advanced eastward across central Illinois between 5am CDT and 9am CDT in a weakening state: however, scattered wind damage was observed.  One of the hardest hit locations was Effingham, where downburst winds of 70mph created minor structural damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A slow-moving frontal boundary was draped near the I-70 corridor, and several rounds of strong to severe thunderstorms occurred across the middle Tennessee Valley. Much of this activity remained south of central and southeastern Illinois, but during the evening an intense bowing segment developed and tracked between US-50 and I-64, producing wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees and power poles were blown down from Xenia east to Highway 45.

On the morning of May 24th a low pressure system was located over the eastern Dakotas, with Illinois positioned in the warm sector. Over the course of the day, several rounds of thunderstorms occurred. First, a squall line tracked across Iowa and reached Illinois by the morning. The most intense part of this squall line tracked north of central Illinois, but the system did produce one instance of wind damage in Stark County. There was a lull in severe weather during the mid-to-late morning, but around midday the lingering storms began to strengthen, while new clusters of storms developed along the associated outflow boundary. These storms continued throughout the afternoon. A cold front moved into the area during the evening, resulting in additional scattered storm development, however, all of the storms weakened into the late evening as daytime heating was lost. Collectively, the thunderstorms on May 24th produced scattered wind damage, hail, and flash flooding across central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were blown down on Main Street in Newman.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the afternoon of May 21st: however, these cells dissipated as they crossed the Mississippi River into a much drier environment across central Illinois.  Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed further east during the evening and were able to transport higher momentum air aloft down to the surface.  Gradient wind gusts were generally 40-50mph, but sporadic gusts as high as 55-65mph were reported in and near the developing showers and storms.  As a result, scattered wind damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large seed boxes were toppled and several tree branches were blown down on a farm south of Moweaqua.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms moved across parts of northwest Illinois during the evening of May 21st producing scattered wind damage.||High winds developed across northern Illinois during the evening of May 21st and continued into the early morning of May 22nd also producing wind damage, widespread in some areas. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the afternoon of May 21st: however, these cells dissipated as they crossed the Mississippi River into a much drier environment across central Illinois.  Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed further east during the evening and were able to transport higher momentum air aloft down to the surface.  Gradient wind gusts were generally 40-50mph, but sporadic gusts as high as 55-65mph were reported in and near the developing showers and storms.  As a result, scattered wind damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were blown down and some siding was torn off a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of May 24th a low pressure system was located over the eastern Dakotas, with Illinois positioned in the warm sector. Over the course of the day, several rounds of thunderstorms occurred. First, a squall line tracked across Iowa and reached Illinois by the morning. The most intense part of this squall line tracked north of central Illinois, but the system did produce one instance of wind damage in Stark County. There was a lull in severe weather during the mid-to-late morning, but around midday the lingering storms began to strengthen, while new clusters of storms developed along the associated outflow boundary. These storms continued throughout the afternoon. A cold front moved into the area during the evening, resulting in additional scattered storm development, however, all of the storms weakened into the late evening as daytime heating was lost. Collectively, the thunderstorms on May 24th produced scattered wind damage, hail, and flash flooding across central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The road was impassable between 1000E and 1100E.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the afternoon of May 21st: however, these cells dissipated as they crossed the Mississippi River into a much drier environment across central Illinois.  Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed further east during the evening and were able to transport higher momentum air aloft down to the surface.  Gradient wind gusts were generally 40-50mph, but sporadic gusts as high as 55-65mph were reported in and near the developing showers and storms.  As a result, scattered wind damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter estimated a wind gust of 65mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of May 24th a low pressure system was located over the eastern Dakotas, with Illinois positioned in the warm sector. Over the course of the day, several rounds of thunderstorms occurred. First, a squall line tracked across Iowa and reached Illinois by the morning. The most intense part of this squall line tracked north of central Illinois, but the system did produce one instance of wind damage in Stark County. There was a lull in severe weather during the mid-to-late morning, but around midday the lingering storms began to strengthen, while new clusters of storms developed along the associated outflow boundary. These storms continued throughout the afternoon. A cold front moved into the area during the evening, resulting in additional scattered storm development, however, all of the storms weakened into the late evening as daytime heating was lost. Collectively, the thunderstorms on May 24th produced scattered wind damage, hail, and flash flooding across central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wall of a machine shed was blown down resulting in another wall and the roof to partially collapse.

Scattered thunderstorms moved across parts of northern Illinois during the afternoon of May 7th producing large hail, localized flooding and one tornado.  Additional scattered thunderstorms developed during the evening of May 7th producing locally severe winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado that touched down in Boone County crossed into McHenry County just north of Perkins Road.  The tornado produced scattered tree damage along its path.  A barn collaspsed along Weidner Road. Several sheep were inside the barn when the tornado struck and two of the sheep were killed.  It is at this location where the tornado was likely at peak intensity with estimated wind speeds of 85 mph.  The tornado continued moving northeast and crossed into Walworth County in southern Wisconsin west of Lilja Road where a silo and barn were damaged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across Iowa and Missouri during the afternoon of May 21st: however, these cells dissipated as they crossed the Mississippi River into a much drier environment across central Illinois.  Additional scattered showers and thunderstorms developed further east during the evening and were able to transport higher momentum air aloft down to the surface.  Gradient wind gusts were generally 40-50mph, but sporadic gusts as high as 55-65mph were reported in and near the developing showers and storms.  As a result, scattered wind damage was reported across the area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was blown down onto a house in Washington due to a non-thunderstorm wind gust.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper-level disturbance interacting with a stationary frontal boundary draped across central Illinois triggered clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms during the afternoon and evening of May 19th.  Some of the cells produced flash flooding and hail as large as ping pong balls near Long Creek in Macon County, while others caused isolated flash flooding in parts of rural Douglas County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water was flowing across the road in the 2200 block of Esther Avenue due to 1 to 3 inches of rain falling in a short amount of time.

On the morning of May 24th a low pressure system was located over the eastern Dakotas, with Illinois positioned in the warm sector. Over the course of the day, several rounds of thunderstorms occurred. First, a squall line tracked across Iowa and reached Illinois by the morning. The most intense part of this squall line tracked north of central Illinois, but the system did produce one instance of wind damage in Stark County. There was a lull in severe weather during the mid-to-late morning, but around midday the lingering storms began to strengthen, while new clusters of storms developed along the associated outflow boundary. These storms continued throughout the afternoon. A cold front moved into the area during the evening, resulting in additional scattered storm development, however, all of the storms weakened into the late evening as daytime heating was lost. Collectively, the thunderstorms on May 24th produced scattered wind damage, hail, and flash flooding across central Illinois.

A long-lasting complex of severe thunderstorms, known as a derecho, tracked from central Nebraska through northwest Illinois during the late night hours May 23rd, to the morning of May 24th. This resulted in frequent wind damage reports and embedded tornadoes. There were 16 tornadoes confirmed in the NWS Quad Cities warning area. The four strongest tornadoes all had maximum winds near 110 mph (EF1), and those were the Delta, IA; Wellman to Frytown, IA; Bennett to Lowden, IA; and Coyne Center, IL tornadoes. This was the most tornadoes in a single event for the NWS Quad Cities warning area since the March 31, 2023 outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 6 inch diameter tree was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Loosely organized multi-cell thunderstorms moved into central Missouri and intensified during the afternoon of the 2nd. One thunderstorm became severe and produced up to half dollar sized hail and wind damage in Adams County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Broadcast media relayed reports of medium sized tree branches snapped. There was also a report of power poles broken. Both reports are from the south side of Quincy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms fired along a cold front on the afternoon of the 4th. A high instability and low shear environment yielded disorganized convection. Regardless, one thunderstorm did produce severe wind gusts in Madison County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The mesonet site recorded a wind gust of 64 mph. No damage was reported nearby.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Two severe storms tracked across McDonough County during the late afternoon and early evening of May 2, 2024.  The first one produced large hail up to golf ball size.  The second storm tracking nearly across the same areas produced large hail and some damaging winds. Heavy rains with the two storms resulted in some minor flooding of some creeks with some shallow water flowing across some county roadways.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Quarter to ping pong ball sized hail report received on social media.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of May 26, 2024, ten tornadoes touched down in the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois from a mix of supercells and QLCS modes. This occurred alongside numerous reports of large hail of up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging winds of up to 81 mph. Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs, some as large as 12 inches in diameter, were downed. A 3 foot diameter tree snapped and fell on a home in Aviston. A power pole also snapped near New Baden where siding was also damaged. Cities impacted include Trenton, Damiansville, Albers, Germantown, and Breese.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following the departure of rain and a cold frontal passage, Southern Illinois had clearing skies and calming winds for the overnight hours early on the 30th. Dense fog developed around midnight, remaining until mid-morning.

Surface low pressure was drifting across the Quad State on the 14th. Showers and thunderstorms popped up in the afternoon to evening hours with the strongest storm in Southern Illinois moving through Marion, producing damage to vinyl fencing and minor street flooding. CAPE values around 1000 J/kg dropped off after sunset. Lapse rates were meager, around 6 C/km, and shear was also limited to around 25 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple vinyl fences blown over at the corner of Cardinal Dr and Mulligan Dr. Pieces of vinyl embedded in neighbor's siding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A long-lasting complex of severe thunderstorms, known as a derecho, tracked from central Nebraska through northwest Illinois during the late night hours May 23rd, to the morning of May 24th. This resulted in frequent wind damage reports and embedded tornadoes. There were 16 tornadoes confirmed in the NWS Quad Cities warning area. The four strongest tornadoes all had maximum winds near 110 mph (EF1), and those were the Delta, IA; Wellman to Frytown, IA; Bennett to Lowden, IA; and Coyne Center, IL tornadoes. This was the most tornadoes in a single event for the NWS Quad Cities warning area since the March 31, 2023 outbreak.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail was slightly bigger than quarters for 5 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Early morning, the surface low was located just north of St. Louis, Missouri, lifting to the NNE as it rapidly deepened. For Southern Illinois, early morning effective bulk shear was 60-65 kts, with a very high 20-30 kts of 0-1 km shear, while SRHs near the Ohio River were around 150-200 m2/s2. MLCAPE was 1000 J/kg, and mid-level lapse rates were 6.5 C/km. A 70-90 kt mid-level jet was west of the Quad State. A strong capping inversion suppressed storm activity in the evening, but the strong lift and upper level diffluence won out early morning. Two lines of storms developed after midnight in Southeast Missouri. The first progressed through the I-64 corridor from 0100-0400 CST. The latter was a very potent thunderstorm line racing eastward early morning April 2nd. This line continued throughout the day all the way to West Virginia, being designated a derecho. For Southern Illinois, widespread wind damage occurred with this line, and sections of the line that took a north-south orientation had a much more favorable line-normal component of shear promoting QLCS tornado development. Seven tornadoes originated in Southern Illinois: 2 in Williamson County (EF-2 and EF-1), 1 in Saline County (EF-2), 3 in Gallatin County (all EF-1s), and 1 in White County (EF-2 with the EF-2 damage in Posey County Indiana). Two people suffered laceration injuries due to flying debris with the Saline County tornado. A total of 19 tornadoes were found during this event, the highest single day total for the NWS Paducah forecast area since Halloween 2003 (also 19).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-2 (peak of EF-1 in White County) tornado began in far eastern White County during the early morning hours of April 2nd. The path began near IL 1400 with tree damage. The tornado tracked eastward, crossing the Wabash River into Posey County, IN. Estimated peak winds on the White County portion of the path were 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area. ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch.  The highest totals fell in McDonough and Warren counties in Illinois where amounts between 2 and 3 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The ASOS had the Moline-Quad Cities Airport measured a thunderstorm wind gust of 58 knots.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Illinois on June 5th, and in its wake, breezy northwest winds gusted 30 to 35 mph during the afternoon of June 6th. This resulted in widespread reports of blowing dust reducing visibility across central and eastern Illinois. The reduced visibility necessitated closures of I-55 and Route 66 in parts of Logan and McLean County for several hours during the afternoon and evening, but no notable car accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reduced visibility from blowing dust led to the closure of I-55 and Route 66 from south of the McLean-Logan County border northward to near Shirley for several hours during the afternoon and evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across north-central Illinois during the evening of June 25th.  Some of the cells produced wind gusts of around 60 mph, with one storm dropping golf ball-sized hail in Altona in Knox County.  Excessive rainfall rates from another storm created localized flash flooding in Canton in Fulton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down on Route 93 in Bradford.

Scattered showers developed during the late afternoon and early evening along a slow-moving cold front that was draped from northern Indiana to near the I-72 corridor in central Illinois. Trained spotters reported that many of these showers exhibited weak rotation and brief funnel clouds, and one produced a landspout tornado in Vermilion County, which eventually tracked into Indiana. This tornado was on the ground for a total of 15 minutes and was rated EF0 after producing minor tree damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A storm chaser monitored a landspout tornado that maintained connection to the ground for a total of 15 minutes along a track from eastern Vermilion County in Illinois to northwestern Warren County in Indiana. The landspout tornado crossed the Illinois-Indiana state line at 1723 CST. Video from the storm chaser captured minor damage to tree branches, resulting in an EF0 rating.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 8 to 10-inch diameter 20-foot long tree branch was blown down on Bowman Avenue.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches were blown down throughout town, including some that fell onto power lines.  One large tree was blown over.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along the nose of the nocturnal low-level jet in eastern Nebraska during the late evening of July 29th...then tracked southeastward across south-central Illinois during the morning of July 30th.  The storms produced sporadic wind damage along and south of a Macomb...to Lincoln...to Lawrenceville line.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 6-inch diameter tree limbs were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm cluster that developed across western Iowa during the pre-dawn hours of July 31st tracked eastward through central Illinois in a decaying fashion during the morning.  The storms produced a few pockets of minor wind damage and reports of quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several large tree branches were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area. ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch.  The highest totals fell in McDonough and Warren counties in Illinois where amounts between 2 and 3 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A trained spotter reported larges trees that were either split by the wind or had large branches downed. Large metal outbuilding destroyed.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a large tree that was snapped, with multiple large branches downed by the winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) that formed over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the pre-dawn hours of July 30th decayed as it tracked southeastward toward the Mississippi River.  As it moved further southeastward, it re-intensified across south-central Illinois during peak heating.  The newly formed cluster of storms produced scattered wind damage and a few hailstones as large as quarters along and south of a Lincoln...to Mattoon...to Lawrenceville line during the afternoon of July 30th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down near the intersection of East IL-250 and West North Avenue on the west side of Olney.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A thunderstorm cluster that developed across western Iowa during the pre-dawn hours of July 31st tracked eastward through central Illinois in a decaying fashion during the morning.  The storms produced a few pockets of minor wind damage and reports of quarter-sized hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A 14-inch diameter tree limb was blown down onto powerlines causing a neighborhood to lose power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Rain that fell during the month of June across Minnesota and Wisconsin caused rises on the Mississippi River, along the Iowa and Illinois border. Additionally, heavy rain that fell locally in July helped to prolong the crest and delay the river falling below flood stage, especially south of the Wapsipinicon, Rock, and Iowa Rivers.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River gage at Dubuque Lock and Dam rose above its major flood stage of 20.5 feet around 6 PM CST on July 4 and crested at 20.85 feet on July 6.  The river fell back below major flood stage around 1 AM  CST on July 9.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Mississippi River Lock and Dam 12 in Bellevue rose above its major flood stage of 20.0 feet around noon on July 5th and at 20.17 feet on July 7. The river fell back below flood stage around 2 AM on July 9.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado was captured on video near the shore of the Mississippi River in Muscatine County Iowa. The tornado crossed the Mississippi River and moved onshore in Rock Island County Illinois, just west of the Loud Thunder Forest Preserve, where it uprooted trees and downed large branches. No rating is assigned for the path of the tornado over the Mississippi River. Once onshore, the tornado was rated EF-0 with maximum winds around 75 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Calm conditions allowed for a surface inversion to develop during the pre-dawn hours on the 4th. Sufficient moisture resulted in the formation of dense fog in far southern counties in Southern Illinois near the Ohio/Mississippi confluence, though low clouds limited a more widespread fog coverage/duration.

A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along an advancing warm front during the morning of June 16th.  The storms remained well north of the I-74 corridor northward to I-80.  Some of the storms skirted across Stark County, resulting in minor wind damage and hail as large as quarters.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 6-inch diameter tree limbs were blown down near the Toulon Sheriffs Office.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across north-central Illinois during the evening of June 25th.  Some of the cells produced wind gusts of around 60 mph, with one storm dropping golf ball-sized hail in Altona in Knox County.  Excessive rainfall rates from another storm created localized flash flooding in Canton in Fulton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Street flooding occurred in a low-lying spot in Canton.  A vehicle became stranded in the high water.

A line of organized storms surged into eastern Iowa late in the day and produced severe winds and heavy rainfall. The strongest storms occurred in a swath just south of Interstate 80, including near Iowa City, producing thunderstorm wind gusts of 60 to 80 MPH. There were rainfall amounts of 2 to 3.50 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of 4 inch tree limbs down. The time of the event was estimated using radar.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed just northeast of Hanover in Jo Daviess County, and tracked to the southeast around 50 mph along an intermittent path. The tornado caused significant roof damage to a home east of Hanover that was rated EF-1. The tornado then moved into Carroll County near S. Breyer Road, around 18:02 CST.  The tornado caused EF-1 damage around Lake Carroll, including damage to a garage, gazebo, and boat dock, and numerous trees downed. The tornado then downed trees in Shannon before lifting around 18:16 CST. The tornado was rated EF-1 with maximum winds around 100 mph, in both Jo Daviess and Carroll Counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado developed northwest of Stockton in Jo Daviess County, and moved E/SE along an intermittent 15 mile path, before lifting near Lena in Stephenson County. In Jo Daviess County, the tornado snapped a wooden power pole near the base, snapped tree trunks, and impacted a farmstead, with damage to an outbuilding and grain elevator supports. The most significant damage in Jo Daviess County was rated EF-2, with maximum winds around 115 mph. The tornado crossed into Stephenson County about a third of a mile south of W. Gerbick Road, around 18:01 CST. A public photo showed the tornado near Lena before it dissipated around 18:09 CST. There was no observable damage in Stephenson County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front moved through Illinois on June 5th, and in its wake, breezy northwest winds gusted 30 to 35 mph during the afternoon of June 6th. This resulted in widespread reports of blowing dust reducing visibility across central and eastern Illinois. The reduced visibility necessitated closures of I-55 and Route 66 in parts of Logan and McLean County for several hours during the afternoon and evening, but no notable car accidents were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Reduced visibility from blowing dust led to the closure of I-55 and Route 66 from Exit 133, just north of Lincoln, northward through the Logan-McLean County border for several hours during the afternoon and evening. Blowing dust subsided as winds weakened after sunset.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of strong to severe thunderstorms developed along an advancing warm front during the morning of June 16th.  The storms remained well north of the I-74 corridor northward to I-80.  Some of the storms skirted across Stark County, resulting in minor wind damage and hail as large as quarters.
Isolated thunderstorms produced a landspout tornado near Melvin in Ford County during the afternoon of July 4th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Video and photos showed a slow moving landspout tornado that developed near County Road E 9000N between N 1200E and N 1300E. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area. ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch.  The highest totals fell in McDonough and Warren counties in Illinois where amounts between 2 and 3 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A public report of a a large section of a tree snapped off and fell on a car.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 15, 2024, a derecho brought very strong winds, several tornadoes, and heavy rainfall to a good portion of the Midwest. Thunderstorms initiated over central Iowa during the late afternoon hours then quickly spread east-southeast through southern Wisconsin, the northern half of Illinois, southern Michigan, and the northern/central Indiana. The event produced significant tree damage, some structural damage, and 10 tornadoes within the NWS Lincoln County Warning Area, most of which were rated EF-0 or EF-1. Back building of storms into the morning of July 16, 2024, brought very heavy rainfall and flash flooding near and south of a Galesburg to Sullivan line. The heaviest rain fell over Fulton and Mason counties where 4-8 was reported, necessitating some road closures and producing damage to roadways in Fulton County. One indirect death occurred in Peoria County as part of this thunderstorm event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree branches up to eight inches in diameter were blown down from near Frederick to Sheldon's Grove along Illinois Route 100.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of May 26, 2024, ten tornadoes touched down in the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois from a mix of supercells and QLCS modes. This occurred alongside numerous reports of large hail of up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging winds of up to 81 mph. Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down just northwest of Omega and tracked eastward south of Forbes Lake primarily uprooting and snapping trees along its path and causing minor roof damage to a farm outbuilding on Seavers Road. The tornado then crossed into Clay County (WFO ILX) and strengthened to EF2 intensity. The tornado was rated EF1 in Marion County with a path length of 4.66 miles and a max path width of 500 yards. Overall, the tornado was rated EF2 with a path length of 8.28 miles and a max path width of 500 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

On the evening of July 15, 2024, a derecho brought very strong winds, several tornadoes, and heavy rainfall to a good portion of the Midwest. Thunderstorms initiated over central Iowa during the late afternoon hours then quickly spread east-southeast through southern Wisconsin, the northern half of Illinois, southern Michigan, and the northern/central Indiana. The event produced significant tree damage, some structural damage, and 10 tornadoes within the NWS Lincoln County Warning Area, most of which were rated EF-0 or EF-1. Back building of storms into the morning of July 16, 2024, brought very heavy rainfall and flash flooding near and south of a Galesburg to Sullivan line. The heaviest rain fell over Fulton and Mason counties where 4-8 was reported, necessitating some road closures and producing damage to roadways in Fulton County. One indirect death occurred in Peoria County as part of this thunderstorm event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous large tree branches were blown down.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began two miles east-southeast of Minonk, near a residence on County Road 2000, producing damage to several trees. Sentinel satellite data showed the tornado tracked northeast through several corn fields, producing damage to the corn. The tornado damaged more trees at a residence on County Road 2100, very close to the Woodford-Livingston county line. The tornado then continued into Livingston County, where it produced damage to trees, corn, and utility poles before quickly dissipating after two tenths of a mile. The damage was rated EF-0 in both Woodford and Livingston counties.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began in East Peoria, near Bloomington Road and Pleasant Hill Road. It continued along Interstate 74 toward the north side of Morton where it damaged an industrial complex. The tornado then continued toward the east side of Morton near the high school, snapping several hardwood trees along the way. The tornado continued southeast into rural Tazewell County, mainly north of Mackinaw near Dee Mack Road and toward the American Legion post where siding was ripped off the east side of the building. The tornado dissipated shortly after that time.

Thunderstorms initiated over central Iowa during the late afternoon hours then quickly spread east-southeast through southern Wisconsin, the northern half of Illinois, southern Michigan, and the northern/central Indiana. The event produced significant tree damage, some structural damage, and 10 tornadoes within the NWS Lincoln County Warning Area, most of which were rated EF-0 or EF-1. Back building of storms into the morning of July 16, 2024, brought very heavy rainfall and flash flooding near and south of a Galesburg to Sullivan line. The heaviest rain fell over Fulton and Mason counties where 4-8 was reported, necessitating some road closures and producing damage to roadways in Fulton County. One indirect death occurred in Peoria County as part of this thunderstorm event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree branches were broken.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began three miles northwest of Alta, along west Club Road near the intersection with Route 91. It damaged several trees in that neighborhood. Additional tree damage occurred along west Alta Lane before the tornado dissipated as it reached Alta.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous slow-moving storms occurred on the afternoon of June 25, lasting into the evening. These storms quickly became strong to severe, with multiple becoming supercells, which ended up producing locally heavy rainfall, damaging winds, large hail, and four tornadoes that were generally weak, but still capable of damage. Winds were generally 60 mph or greater with the stronger storms, with a corridor of winds greater than 65 mph. These thunderstorms remained nearly stationary in areas, resulting in nearly 2 to 4 inches of quick rainfall and flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees downed in Memorial Cemetery by the thunderstorm winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 15, 2024, a derecho brought very strong winds, several tornadoes, and heavy rainfall to a good portion of the Midwest. Thunderstorms initiated over central Iowa during the late afternoon hours then quickly spread east-southeast through southern Wisconsin, the northern half of Illinois, southern Michigan, and the northern/central Indiana. The event produced significant tree damage, some structural damage, and 10 tornadoes within the NWS Lincoln County Warning Area, most of which were rated EF-0 or EF-1. Back building of storms into the morning of July 16, 2024, brought very heavy rainfall and flash flooding near and south of a Galesburg to Sullivan line. The heaviest rain fell over Fulton and Mason counties where 4-8 was reported, necessitating some road closures and producing damage to roadways in Fulton County. One indirect death occurred in Peoria County as part of this thunderstorm event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The tornado began in Monica, damaging a couple of roofs and trees in that area. It then progressed south of Princeville, damaging several large trees before dissipating along W Drive south of Princeville.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began two miles west-southwest of Cazenovia, north of Black Partridge Road, and moved southeast producing tree damage at three properties along the road. The worst tree damage was to a property west-northwest of the intersection of Black Partridge Road and Wienman Road. At this property, dozens of trees were damaged. The tornado continued southeast and produced damage to a cornfield. The damage could be seen in Sentinel satellite data. The tornado dissipated about three tenths of a mile southeast of the intersection of Black Partridge Road and Wienman Road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area. ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch.  The highest totals fell in McDonough and Warren counties in Illinois where amounts between 2 and 3 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found a tornado touched down northwest of Taylor Ridge in Rock Island County. The tornado damaged trees, dented two large grain bins, and damaged power poles. The tornado then moved into Mercer County around 18:27 CST, about a third of a mile west of US Route 67 on 190th Ave W. In Mercer County, the tornado damaged trees, power poles, and small farm outbuildings. The tornado then moved into Henry County around 18:36 CST, about 0.4 miles south of Camp Creek Road. The tornado continued to damage trees, power poles, and small farm outbuildings, before crossing Interstate 74 and lifting south of Andover. In all three counties (Rock Island, Mercer, Henry) the tornado was rated an EF-1 with maximum winds around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the morning of the 17th, two 16-year-olds, one boy and one girl, were on a raft near Montrose Beach when strong winds allegedly caused their raft to flip over. A bystander pulled the girl to shore but officials were unable to recover the boy before he drowned. At the time, winds at Montrose harbor were peaking in the 20-23 kt range and the Chicago pier buoy was recording wave heights of 1 to 1.5 ft.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 15, 2024, a derecho brought very strong winds, several tornadoes, and heavy rainfall to a good portion of the Midwest. Thunderstorms initiated over central Iowa during the late afternoon hours then quickly spread east-southeast through southern Wisconsin, the northern half of Illinois, southern Michigan, and the northern/central Indiana. The event produced significant tree damage, some structural damage, and 10 tornadoes within the NWS Lincoln County Warning Area, most of which were rated EF-0 or EF-1. Back building of storms into the morning of July 16, 2024, brought very heavy rainfall and flash flooding near and south of a Galesburg to Sullivan line. The heaviest rain fell over Fulton and Mason counties where 4-8 was reported, necessitating some road closures and producing damage to roadways in Fulton County. One indirect death occurred in Peoria County as part of this thunderstorm event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding resulted in the closure of US-24 south of Lewistown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area. ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch.  The highest totals fell in McDonough and Warren counties in Illinois where amounts between 2 and 3 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS Storm Survey found a tornado touched down northwest of Taylor Ridge in Rock Island County. The tornado damaged trees, dented two large grain bins, and damaged power poles. The tornado then moved into Mercer County around 18:27 CST, about a third of a mile west of US Route 67 on 190th Ave W. In Mercer County, the tornado damaged trees, power poles, and small farm outbuildings. The tornado then moved into Henry County around 18:36 CST, about 0.4 miles south of Camp Creek Road. The tornado continued to damage trees, power poles, and small farm outbuildings, before crossing Interstate 74 and lifting south of Andover. In all three counties (Rock Island, Mercer, Henry) the tornado was rated an EF-1 with maximum winds around 100 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: On the evening of July 15, 2024, a derecho brought very strong winds, several tornadoes, and heavy rainfall to a good portion of the Midwest. Thunderstorms initiated over central Iowa during the late afternoon hours then quickly spread east-southeast through southern Wisconsin, the northern half of Illinois, southern Michigan, and the northern/central Indiana. The event produced significant tree damage, some structural damage, and 10 tornadoes within the NWS Lincoln County Warning Area, most of which were rated EF-0 or EF-1. Back building of storms into the morning of July 16, 2024, brought very heavy rainfall and flash flooding near and south of a Galesburg to Sullivan line. The heaviest rain fell over Fulton and Mason counties where 4-8 was reported, necessitating some road closures and producing damage to roadways in Fulton County. One indirect death occurred in Peoria County as part of this thunderstorm event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell onto an RV that was parked on a property off Cedar Bluff Road near the Spoon River south of Elmore. Nearly a week later, on July 23, 2024, a 77-year old man from New York was reported missing after last being seen in his RV. Law enforcement found that his cell phone last used a phone tower near Elmore, and began search operations. The man was found later on July 23 in the brush near the Spoon River, in the vicinity of the damaged RV. He was still breathing but unresponsive, and despite life-saving medical efforts he succumbed to his ailments and was pronounced deceased on July 23.

Thunderstorms initiated over central Iowa during the late afternoon hours then quickly spread east-southeast through southern Wisconsin, the northern half of Illinois, southern Michigan, and the northern/central Indiana. The event produced significant tree damage, some structural damage, and 10 tornadoes within the NWS Lincoln County Warning Area, most of which were rated EF-0 or EF-1. Back building of storms into the morning of July 16, 2024, brought very heavy rainfall and flash flooding near and south of a Galesburg to Sullivan line. The heaviest rain fell over Fulton and Mason counties where 4-8 was reported, necessitating some road closures and producing damage to roadways in Fulton County. One indirect death occurred in Peoria County as part of this thunderstorm event.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four to six inch tree limbs were blown down and power outages occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms rapidly developed over northern Iowa during the afternoon and organized into a powerful line of wind producing storms that surged through the Quad Cities and into northern and central Illinois through the evening hours.  These storms brought widespread damaging winds up to 100 mph for many locations, extending across eastern Iowa, much of Illinois, and into Indiana.  The Storm Prediction Center has classified this event as a Derecho which by definition is a swath of wind damage at least 400 miles long and at least 60 miles wide including several well-separated significant wind gusts over 75 mph.  Corridors of significant wind damage occurred along Highway 20. 10 tornadoes also occurred with these storms in the NWS Quad Cities service area. ||In addition to the wind, very heavy rainfall fell in a short amount of time with many locations seeing over an inch.  The highest totals fell in McDonough and Warren counties in Illinois where amounts between 2 and 3 inches fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Nearly continuous damage was surveyed consistent with a tornado producing 85-100 mph winds. In Davenport, Iowa, damage occurred to dozens of large trees, including several large healthy snapped trees. Some trees fell on homes and at least one on a car. A large section of an apartment roof was also torn off. Damage in Scott County Iowa was rated EF1 with maximum winds around 100 mph. The tornado crossed the Mississippi River and moved into Rock Island County Illinois along River Drive in East Moline, around 18:28 CST. The tornado snapped some tree trunks before dissipating around 18:32 CST. The tornado was also rated EF-1 in Rock Island County, with maximum winds around 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe thunderstorms developed in the afternoon of June 13th ahead of an approaching cold front. Supercells initially produced large hail in northeast Missouri before transitioning into mostly a wind threat as they crossed the Mississippi River into Adams and Brown Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Adams County emergency manager reported farm structures down, power poles snapped, wires down, and trees snapped and uprooted near Highway 96 and Highway 24 north of Quincy. There were also multiple large trees down and some roof damage to farm outbuildings near 24th Street and Boy Scout Road.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: This tornado began four miles west of Dunlap in a wooded area northwest of the corner of west Legion Hall Road and north Mendenhall Road. It moved southeast where it produced significant tree damage on five different properties, and one homestead lost 40 trees. Many of the trees were very tall pine trees which were snapped. The tornado continued southeast, knocking a tree onto power lines along Evans Mill Road. It produced more tree damage along Fuecht Road, and then lifted in a field east of the road about two miles west-southwest of Dunlap.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: In the early afternoon of June 25th, a cold front moved into the forecast area from the north, bringing with it a broken QLCS. Two thunderstorms within the QLCS produced severe hail and wind damage in Brown County. No injuries or fatalities were reported.

In the early afternoon of June 25th, a cold front moved into the forecast area from the north, bringing with it a broken QLCS. Two thunderstorms within the QLCS produced severe hail and wind damage in Brown County. No injuries or fatalities were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Brown County emergency manager reported power poles and trees down over the north side of Versailles.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable air mass across the region ahead of a southward sinking cold front resulted in thunderstorm development, with several being severe. These produced large hail and scattered damaging winds. Most hail reports ranged in size from quarter to golf ball size hail. The largest hail fell in Van Buren County near Keosauqua where 2 to 3 inch diameter hail fell. There were also a few reports of 60 to 80 MPH across far southeast Iowa into west central Illinois, which damaged trees and knocked down several outbuildings.  The most widespread damage occurred near Oakville, IA.  Two brief, non-damaging tornadoes were also seen north of Macomb, IL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado was observed in an open field between Bushnell and Bardolph. There was no observed damage with the tornado, and therefore has been assigned an EF-Unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of severe thunderstorms dropped south across northern Illinois during the evening and night of the 22nd. The line produced four EF-0 tornadoes in addition to widespread wind damage across the Chicago metro. Many also saw torrential rainfall and localized flash flooding, particularly in extreme northeast Illinois north of the I-90 corridor where a widespread 3-6 of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station EW1412 1 recorded 3.17 of rain.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A brief tornado touched down near Fairview Avenue and 38th Street in Oak Brook. The first damage was noted along Ridgewood Court and 38th Street, where multiple trees had large branches broken. The tornado moved generally to the southeast, entering Downers Grove near Cumnor Road and 39th Street, where damage was noted to numerous trees. Minor damage was also noted to the cupola of a nearby residence. The tornado moved through the Longmeadow neighborhood, continuing to damage numerous trees. In some areas, the most significant tree damage was noted just above roof level. The tornado slightly intensified near 41st Street and Williams Street, where a few homes had minor roofing damage and numerous trees had large branches broken. Debris from damaged fences was blown in a somewhat convergent pattern toward 41st Street on opposite sides of the street. The tornado appeared to dissipate just southeast of this area as it approached Westmont. The peak wind of this tornado was estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective vortex produced windy thunderstorms as it moved across northern Illinois during the afternoon of the 16th. These storms developed a gust front which managed to kick up dust and significantly reduce visibilities across parts of Kane County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few 3 to 5 inch diameter tree limbs were downed in Polo.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: One lone thunderstorm on the morning of the 13th produced severe winds over portions of the Chicago metro. Additional clusters of strong to severe storms developed that  afternoon and evening mainly impacting areas south of I-80 with severe winds and one tornado.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An NWS damage survey confirmed an EF-1 tornado with peak winds around 110 mph began in McLean County and crossed into Ford County before lifting just northwest of Gibson City. While in the WFO Chicago CWA, the tornado produced mostly tree and power line damage in addition to damage to a few grain bins.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Four people required rescuing while swimming near Lighthouse Beach during the afternoon of the 30th. Three of the four swimmers, two teenage girls and one adult woman, were rescued. One of the teenagers was transported to a hospital where she was treated and released while the others did not require hospital treatment. The fourth swimmer, a 41 year old man, could not be recovered. Rescue efforts continued into the next day but officials called off the search that afternoon when they were still unable to locate the man. His body was found 5 days later on July 5th by a fishing charter roughly two miles offshore from Dempster Street Beach. At the time of the incident, a red no swimming flag was flying at the beach, no lifeguards were on duty, and a water closed, no swimming sign was sitting at the entrance of the beach. A beach hazards statement and small craft advisory were in effect at the time advertising waves up to 8 feet.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Mostly clear skies and plenty of moisture surging in from the south allowed for heat indices to reach 105 to 112 degrees across Southern Illinois. The highest observed heat index in Southern Illinois was 112 degrees at the Dixon Springs RAWS site. A line of thunderstorms advancing southward through the afternoon hours brought an end to heat concerns for the day with the passage of an outflow boundary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A warm, moist, and unstable air mass across the region ahead of a southward sinking cold front resulted in thunderstorm development, with several being severe. These produced large hail and scattered damaging winds. Most hail reports ranged in size from quarter to golf ball size hail. The largest hail fell in Van Buren County near Keosauqua where 2 to 3 inch diameter hail fell. There were also a few reports of 60 to 80 MPH across far southeast Iowa into west central Illinois, which damaged trees and knocked down several outbuildings.  The most widespread damage occurred near Oakville, IA.  Two brief, non-damaging tornadoes were also seen north of Macomb, IL.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large branch broken off of a hickory tree.

An iron worker died and one other was injured after scaffolding collapsed in Chicago. Officials say two men were working on the University of Chicago cancer pavilion when high winds caused the scaffolding to collapse leaving the two to fall eight stories. One of the men had died on site while the other was transported to a hospital in critical condition, but survived. Winds during the day were regularly gusting between 40 and 45 mph around the city with a peak wind gust of 51 mph measured at Midway Airport that afternoon.
During the afternoon of the 20th, a line of showers and thunderstorms produced a narrow corridor of very heavy rainfall in the Sauk Valley area of northern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Private weather KILDIXON53 recorded 2.71 inches of rain in an hour and a half's time. 2.28 inches of that fell in 40 minutes with over an inch being recorded in a 15 minute span.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho moved across northern Illinois during the evening of July 15th producing widespread wind damage and 30 tornadoes in northeast Illinois.  One of these tornadoes crossed into northwest Indiana.  At the height of the storms, 430,000 customers lost power with some power not restored for four days, on July 19th.

Thunderstorms developed during the evening of July 14th and continued into the early morning of July 15th, as they moved across all of northern and central Illinois.  Nine tornadoes occurred, along with widespread wind damage.  In the City of Chicago, there were 2,810 tree emergency calls and 1,430 calls for tree debris. A line of severe thunderstorms dropped south across northern Illinois during the evening and night of the 22nd. The line produced four EF-0 tornadoes in addition to widespread wind damage across the Chicago metro. Many also saw torrential rainfall and localized flash flooding, particularly in extreme northeast Illinois north of the I-90 corridor where a widespread 3-6 of rain fell.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Mesonet station DW7142 recorded 3.8 of rain.
