EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A derecho moved across northern Illinois during the evening of July 15th producing widespread wind damage and 30 tornadoes in northeast Illinois.  One of these tornadoes crossed into northwest Indiana.  At the height of the storms, 430,000 customers lost power with some power not restored for four days, on July 19th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado with estimated peak winds of 95 mph touched down just south of Interstate 290 near Francisco Avenue and moved east, until it turned northeast and crossed Interstate 290 between Damen Avenue and Ashland Avenue.  Tree damage occurred along the path of the tornado.  Minor roof damage occurred to a building on Flournoy Street.  More extensive tree damage including healthy trees uprooted occurred near Rush and Stroger Hospitals.  After crossing Interstate 290, the tornado turned east near Skinner Park where the most significant damage occurred.  Numerous large healthy trees were uprooted, one falling on a fence.  Roof damage occurred to a building in Skinner Park and the Chicago Police Academy building sustained minor fascia damage.  The tornado was likely at peak intensity in this area.  The tornado continued producing tree damage, crossed Interstate 90/94 and then ended before reaching the Chicago River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the night on May 6th, 2024, a line of severe thunderstorms moved across western Missouri and impacted the NWS St. Louis forecast area during the early morning of May 7th. A few instances of damaging straight-line wind was observed across parts of west-central and southwest Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A machine shed was damaged with debris spread across the farm field. Also several large tree limbs were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flooding across parts of northeast Illinois during the late morning and early afternoon of September 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Rainfall of 2.30 inches was reported in 30 minutes.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flooding across parts of northeast Illinois during the late morning and early afternoon of September 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station measured almost 2 inches of rain in 30 minutes with a storm total of 2.37 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flooding across parts of northeast Illinois during the late morning and early afternoon of September 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station reported a storm total of 3.27 inches of rain with just over 1.50 inches falling in 30 minutes ending 1130 AM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flooding across parts of northeast Illinois during the late morning and early afternoon of September 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A private weather station reported a storm total rainfall of 2.91 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the afternoon and early evening of May 26, 2024, ten tornadoes touched down in the St. Louis metropolitan area and southwest Illinois from a mix of supercells and QLCS modes. This occurred alongside numerous reports of large hail of up to 2.5 inches in diameter and damaging winds of up to 81 mph. Thankfully, there were no reported injuries or fatalities with these thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down north of Carlyle and tracked eastward significantly damaging a garage and shed, removing portions of a roof on a home, and breaking large branches off of trees. This tornado continued on the east side of the Carlyle Lake, where there were reports and photos of power lines down, multiple trees down, at least partial removal of a house's roof, a barn destroyed, and the majority of another barn's roof removed. The tornado was rated EF1 in Clinton County with a path length of 17.64 miles and a max path width of 325 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Upper-level ridging peaked over the forecast area on the 21st. Scattered storms developed in the northeastern part of the Quad State closer to the warm front near a residual boundary. CAPE values near 2000 J/kg and DCAPE values near 1000 J/kg allowed for downdraft development. Effective bulk shear of 25 kts was sufficient for storms, but somewhat limited. Tree damage was observed in White County just west of the bridge over the Wabash River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree debris brought down by thunderstorm downdraft winds was observed on IL-141 west of the Wabash River bridge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A SW to NE oriented heavy rainfall band crossed Southern Illinois during the late evening hours of the 23rd. A second band formed ahead of the first, and then merged together resulting in a localized higher rainfall total swath of 3-5 inches from Southeast Jackson County through Northwest Williamson County into Southern Franklin County. The highest observed rainfall total was a CoCoRaHS site 2 miles ESE of Bush, IL which received 4.89 inches. Flash flooding was observed in West Frankfort while areas of standing water were observed further west-southwest. Severe weather potential did not end up materializing with this line, likely due to insufficient clearing and poor mid-level lapse rates.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple social media posts and comments reported flash flooding in and around West Frankfort. Radar and rain gauge reports suggest approximately 2 inches of rain in under 90 minutes as two lines of heavy rain merged.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The remnants of Hurricane Helene made their way through the Quad State region with heavy rainfall in Southern Illinois of 3 to 6 inches with the highest totals located in the southeastern counties: Saline, Gallatin, and Hardin. Despite the high rainfall totals, hourly rainfall rates remained low enough to keep flooding issues from developing. Several strong wind gusts of 40+ mph were observed and tree damage was reported as saturated soils made trees more susceptible.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few trees were downed in the Benton, IL area with noted locations including DuQuoin Street and I-57 NB near mile marker 72. Heavy rain through the day saturated soils and made trees more susceptible to wind damage.

A 22 year old man went swimming in Lake Michigan early in the morning of July 1st and went missing.  He was found unresponsive in the water near Montrose Beach.  He was taken to a local hospital where he was pronounced dead.  Waves at the nearby Wilmette buoy were 3 to 5 feet.

A large upper-level ridge of high pressure centered over the Midwest brought very hot weather to central Illinois on August 26th and 27th.  High temperatures were mostly in the lower to middle 90s on August 26th, but climbed into the 95-100 degree range on the 27th.  With dewpoints hovering in the upper 70s to near 80 degrees, heat index readings peaked from 105 to 115 degrees along and north of the I-72 corridor during the afternoon of August 27th.  While no heat-related deaths were reported, this was the hottest period of weather across central Illinois during the entire summer of 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings peaked at 115-120 degrees during the afternoon of August 27th...including 119 degrees at the Galesburg Airport.  No heat-related deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flooding across parts of northeast Illinois during the late morning and early afternoon of September 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High water was reported in the right lane of Interstate 90/94 near the Jane Byrne Interchange.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Scattered thunderstorms produced heavy rain and localized flooding across parts of northeast Illinois during the late morning and early afternoon of September 24th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two lanes of eastbound Interstate 80 were blocked by flood waters near Harlem Avenue.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete thunderstorms developed in the late morning of May 24th in the free warm sector ahead of an approaching cold front. Portions of south central Illinois received multiple rounds of thunderstorms as they kept redeveloping behind ongoing convection. Overall, these areas received large hail, damaging winds, and over 8 inches of rain in some locations.

There were several rounds of severe storms on May 7th, 2024. An early morning line of storms brought wind damage to a large portion of Iowa, Illinois, and northeast Missouri.  In the early afternoon, a few storms brought large hail to northwest Illinois, followed by another round of gusty storms during the evening hours.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Report from MPing.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed north of a stationary frontal boundary draped across south-central Illinois during the pre-dawn hours of October 25th.  One cell produced penny-sized hail in Urbana in Champaign County.

A field fire developed south-southeast of Ogden in Champaign County shortly before 3pm on October 29th.  Thanks to very low fuel moisture, relative humidity values less than 30 percent, and strong southerly winds gusting over 40mph, the fire was able to rapidly expand northeastward into Vermilion County and across I-74.  Based on Sentinel-2 satellite data, the fire scar was approximately 2.5 miles in length with a maximum width of about 1 mile.  While no structures were directly impacted, I-74 was closed for a little over 2 hours between the St. Joseph exit (mile marker 192) and Fithian exit (mile marker 200).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A field fire started about 1 mile south-southeast of Ogden between IL-49 and County Road 2800 East shortly before 3pm CDT.  The fire then raced northeastward across open fields before crossing into Vermilion County just east of Ogden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A field fire developed south-southeast of Ogden in Champaign County shortly before 3pm on October 29th.  Thanks to very low fuel moisture, relative humidity values less than 30 percent, and strong southerly winds gusting over 40mph, the fire was able to rapidly expand northeastward into Vermilion County and across I-74.  Based on Sentinel-2 satellite data, the fire scar was approximately 2.5 miles in length with a maximum width of about 1 mile.  While no structures were directly impacted, I-74 was closed for a little over 2 hours between the St. Joseph exit (mile marker 192) and Fithian exit (mile marker 200).
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A field fire crossed from Champaign County into Vermilion County just east of Ogden shortly after 3pm CDT.  The fire crossed I-74 before eventually being contained in an open field.  No structures were impacted: however, I-74 was closed between mile markers 192 and 200 for a little over 2 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the very late evening and early morning hours of October 30th and 31st, a severe QLCS passed through the forecast area. This line produced wind gusts of up to 81 mph east of Quincy. It also produced one tornado during its passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-0 tornado formed at 1130pm CDT just east of South 24th St. off of Lake Dr. North. As it quickly moved eastward, it produced sporadic tree damage and then minor structural in Payson Heights. From there, it continued eastward, producing sporadic tree damage, including several trees uprooted or snapped, before dissipating at Schildt Ln. The tornado had a path length of 0.35 miles and a max path width of 100 yards. No deaths or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the very late evening and early morning hours of October 30th and 31st, a severe QLCS passed through the forecast area. This line produced wind gusts of up to 81 mph east of Quincy. It also produced one tornado during its passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An 81 mph wind gust was measured by the ASOS at KUIN.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Widespread freeze and frost brought an end to the growing season for many locations that did not experience patchy sub-freezing temperatures on the morning of the 16th. Low temperatures in Southern Illinois fell to 28-32 degrees; however, locations near the Wabash Valley remained above freezing, causing the first freeze to fall outside the defined growing season. High pressure had moved directly over the region allowing for clear skies and calm winds.

Severe drought conditions developed across much of northern and northeastern Illinois during the middle and latter parts of October. Precipitation over the area was far below normal for the month. The Rockford Airport recorded just 0.79 of rain for the entire month, 2.63 below their October normal. Chicago-O'Hare fell 2.25 short of normal having recorded 1.18, while the NWS Chicago office in Romeoville recorded 0.95 during October falling 2.72 shy of normal. Numerous wildfires occurred in October around NWS Chicago's county warning area, including one near Herscher in Kankakee County that destroyed over 300 acres worth of crops. The severe drought continued into November.

Severe drought conditions developed across much of northern and northeastern Illinois during the middle and latter parts of October. Precipitation over the area was far below normal for the month. The Rockford Airport recorded just 0.79 of rain for the entire month, 2.63 below their October normal. Chicago-O'Hare fell 2.25 short of normal having recorded 1.18, while the NWS Chicago office in Romeoville recorded 0.95 during October falling 2.72 shy of normal. Numerous wildfires occurred in October around NWS Chicago's county warning area, including one near Herscher in Kankakee County that destroyed over 300 acres worth of crops. The severe drought continued into November.

Widespread freeze and frost brought an end to the growing season for many locations that did not experience patchy sub-freezing temperatures on the morning of the 16th. Low temperatures in Southern Illinois fell to 28-32 degrees; however, locations near the Wabash Valley remained above freezing, causing the first freeze to fall outside the defined growing season. High pressure had moved directly over the region allowing for clear skies and calm winds.

The first patchy freeze of the season along with frost brought an end to the growing season for part of the region. Low temperatures in Southern Illinois were highly variable from 29-32 degrees to 33-37 degrees as the region was on the leading edge of high pressure building in from the west. A cold front had previously moved into the Deep South, resulting in northwesterly winds and cold air advection.

Strong non-thunderstorm winds on the 31st managed to knock down a gas station canopy in Crystal Lake which fell onto two cars. A photo of the damage was shared by emergency management. A wind advisory was in effect at the time advertising wind gusts upwards of 45 mph.

A large wildfire burned 400 acres of cropland northeast of Herscher in Kankakee County during the afternoon of the 6th. According to one online news source, the fire was believed to be started from the rear cropper mechanism of a combine. The same news source lists an estimated loss of $320,000, a majority of which was corn and the rest soy. The fire took several hours and the help of multiple fire crews to extinguish and burned a total of around 400 acres worth of crops; 300 of corn and 100 of soybeans. A Special Weather Statement was in effect at the time advertising an elevated fire danger for the day due to gusty winds and dry fuels, especially south of I-80. Numerous smaller fires broke out across northern Illinois on the 6th.

A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Southern Illinois, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-4 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A broad area of straight-line wind damage, primarily from the rear-flank downdraft of a supercell storm, occurred south of Carrier Mills. Tree branch damage occurred on US Hwy 45 and Tuller Rd while more significant tree damage occurred on Pankey Rd and Taborn Rd with uprooted trees. A large span chicken house collapsed on Walnut Grove Rd.

The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State. ||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-1500 J/kg in S Illinois. Effective bulk shear was 40-50 kts with STP of 1. Precipitable water was 1.5-1.75 in. The morning storms mostly missed just south of S Illinois with some street flooding in Johnston City. ||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 2000-3500 J/kg in S Illinois. For S Illinois, effective bulk shear rose to 50 kts, with SRH around 150-250 m2/s2 and STP of 2-3.5. 0-1 km shear was 20 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1200-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds. ||Three tornadoes occurred in S Illinois. Two of these were with the late afternoon to early evening supercells. An EF-3 in the Lake of Egypt area of Johnson County debarked some trees, damaged several homes in the Eagle Point Bay subdivision, and injured one person. An EF-1 tornado in Johnson and Union Counties ahead of the approaching cold front tracked southeastward to Buncombe. The third tornado, an EF-1, was with the cold front in the Grayville area, tracking into Gibson County, IN. The supercells produced several hail reports, with a 2 inch hail report near the Williamson/Saline County border. Training heavy rain produced significant flash flooding for the second time in a month in Saline County, focused on Harrisburg with U.S. 45 flooded and at least one water rescue. Rainfall amounts in Southern Illinois were 1-4 inches, with the highest amounts in Alexander/Pulaski County and along a line from Williamson to Gallatin County. Numerous trees and power lines were damaged with the supercells and the frontal passage. Late evening power outages amounted to roughly a third of Williamson, Union, Johnson, and Alexander Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado began in far northeastern White County south of Grayville. As it progressed eastward, the tornado damaged numerous trees and produced minor damage to homes in the area. A carport was damaged with damage to vehicles due to debris falling on them. The tornado crossed an offshoot of the Wabash River, reaching the portion of Gibson County, Indiana on Kingdom Island. Peak estimated winds with the White County segment, and the tornado as a whole, were 110 mph.

For S Illinois, effective bulk shear rose to 50 kts, with SRH around 150-250 m2/s2 and STP of 2-3.5. 0-1 km shear was 20 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1200-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds. ||Three tornadoes occurred in S Illinois. Two of these were with the late afternoon to early evening supercells. An EF-3 in the Lake of Egypt area of Johnson County debarked some trees, damaged several homes in the Eagle Point Bay subdivision, and injured one person. An EF-1 tornado in Johnson and Union Counties ahead of the approaching cold front tracked southeastward to Buncombe. The third tornado, an EF-1, was with the cold front in the Grayville area, tracking into Gibson County, IN. The supercells produced several hail reports, with a 2 inch hail report near the Williamson/Saline County border. Training heavy rain produced significant flash flooding for the second time in a month in Saline County, focused on Harrisburg with U.S. 45 flooded and at least one water rescue. Rainfall amounts in Southern Illinois were 1-4 inches, with the highest amounts in Alexander/Pulaski County and along a line from Williamson to Gallatin County. Numerous trees and power lines were damaged with the supercells and the frontal passage. Late evening power outages amounted to roughly a third of Williamson, Union, Johnson, and Alexander Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding occurred in Johnston City.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacting with a seasonably warm and moist environment triggered clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms across central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of August 30th.  One cell dropped nickel-sized hail, while others produced wind gusts of around 60mph and minor wind damage.  In addition, urban flash flooding occurred across portions of Springfield and Champaign.  No road closures or water rescues were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A power pole was snapped and a tree was blown across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front triggered a line of strong thunderstorms during the morning of August 1st.  One cluster of minor wind damage occurred across portions of Sangamon and Logan counties during the pre-dawn hours while another occurred further southeast across Clay and Richland counties toward midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several power poles were blown down near Marigold Lane and U.S. 45.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacting with a highly unstable airmass triggered a line of severe thunderstorms across west-central Illinois during the evening of August 15th.  The storms swept across Knox County, northern Peoria County, and Stark County...producing scattered wind damage and hail as large as quarters.  One cell spawned a brief EF-1 tornado northwest of West Jersey in Stark County.  The tornado caused mainly tree damage before quickly dissipating.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree tops were broken off.

A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Southern Illinois, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-4 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Straight-line wind damage on the north end of a tornado track was found along Blue Hole Rd, Feazel Rd, Butler Rd, and Willow Grove Rd. Tree damage generally fell towards the south or southeast. The most significant damage was an uprooted tree which fell onto a house.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado impacted the the community of Cora in extreme western Jackson County, IL. The tornado began south of Cora where it uprooted a tree in the Mississippi River bottomlands and did roof damage to a nearby barn. One manufactured home slid about a foot off its foundation. One person caught outside by the tornado was injured by a falling tree limb. The tornado ended immediately north of Cora. Peak winds with this tornado were estimated at 108 mph.

Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-4 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A report was submitted via mPING of flooding in Carbondale. Hourly rainfall rates peaked at a radar estimated 1 to 1.75 inches within an hour in the Carbondale area between 1340-1440CST with MRMS unit flow values peaking around 1430CST.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-3 tornado began north of Goreville, IL, where a security camera at a house on Newton Lane captured the tornado's formation. Crossing I-57, the tornado snapped large tree limbs and damaged an outbuilding on Pulley's Mill Ln. The tornado was again filmed crossing I-24 northeast of Goreville. Along IL-37, the tornado did roof damage to two homes and snapped a few wooden power poles. Nearby trees suffered extreme damage, with debarking noted on a few trees near the intersection of IL-37 and Orchared Ln, along with numerous large trees snapped. To the east on Orchard Ln, a home suffered a direct hit by the tornado, with the roof being removed and most of the exterior walls collapsed. A nearby outbuilding was also destroyed, along with several large snapped trees. As the tornado approached the Eagle Point Bay subdivision on Lake of Egypt, it snapped several large trees and wooden electrical poles on Deaton Rd and Greentree Dr. Two manufactured homes were severely damaged or destroyed. One resident here suffered broken bone injuries. Another house had its roof uplifted slightly and was slightly moved off its foundation. Continuing east-southeast into the Eagle Point Bay subdivision, the tornado weakened then dissipated, doing mainly tree damage and minor roof damage to homes along and near John Able Dr and Thunderhead Dr. Peak winds with this tornado were estimated at 150 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacting with a highly unstable airmass triggered a line of severe thunderstorms across west-central Illinois during the evening of August 15th.  The storms swept across Knox County, northern Peoria County, and Stark County...producing scattered wind damage and hail as large as quarters.  One cell spawned a brief EF-1 tornado northwest of West Jersey in Stark County.  The tornado caused mainly tree damage before quickly dissipating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An advancing warm front triggered a line of strong thunderstorms during the morning of August 1st.  One cluster of minor wind damage occurred across portions of Sangamon and Logan counties during the pre-dawn hours while another occurred further southeast across Clay and Richland counties toward midday.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A large tree was split at the base and an 8-inch diameter branch was blown down across Elm Street.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down along Walnut Creek near the Knox County-Stark County line about 3 miles northwest of West Jersey at 11:06pm CDT.  It moved east-northeastward through farm fields before crossing over Walnut Creek again and snapping trees as it progressed across 250 East Road.  The tornado then crossed State Route 78 and dissipated in a field at 11:12pm CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The end of July heat event continued through midday on August 1st, at which point a line of storms moved through the I-64 corridor, resulting in cooler and drier conditions afterwards. Isolated storms developed late afternoon into the evening. The highest heat indices on the 1st in Southern Illinois were 112F observations at the Crab Orchard RAWS site, the Dixon Springs RAWS site, and the Cairo Regional Airport AWOS. Some counties reached excessive heat warning criteria by meeting heat advisory criteria 4 consecutive days.

A robust squall line swept through the I-64 corridor of Southern Illinois during the early afternoon hours on the 1st. Numerous wind damage reports were received, primarily in Wayne, Hamilton, and White Counties with many trees and powerlines damaged along with several farm structures. Utility data indicated that roughly half of Wayne County lost power with this storm line. MLCAPE values were approximately 3000 J/kg, DCAPE around 1000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 30 kts. Scattered showers and storms continued through the evening and overnight as a cold front moved through during the night, with storm activity in the later convection remaining sub-severe in Southern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor street flooding occurred in Mount Vernon as a quick inch of rainfall, based on radar estimates, fell within an hour.

Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Tree limb damage was reported via mPING north of Equality as a storm progressed east-northeastward through Gallatin County.

A robust squall line swept through the I-64 corridor of Southern Illinois during the early afternoon hours on the 1st. Numerous wind damage reports were received, primarily in Wayne, Hamilton, and White Counties with many trees and powerlines damaged along with several farm structures. Utility data indicated that roughly half of Wayne County lost power with this storm line. MLCAPE values were approximately 3000 J/kg, DCAPE around 1000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 30 kts. Scattered showers and storms continued through the evening and overnight as a cold front moved through during the night, with storm activity in the later convection remaining sub-severe in Southern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A powerful line of storms surged southeastward through northeastern Hamilton County causing extensive damage.  Large sections of metal roofing from a long farm building blew off at County Rd 1300 E, while a smaller metal roof section blew off at County Rd 1700 N/County Rd 1440 E. A row of 10 power lines snapped on Blairsville Rd/County Rd 1800 N. West of Bungay, several trees were blown down in a diverging pattern. On County Rd 1800 N, southeast of Bungay, a large section of roofing was removed from a farm building with a side wall fallen over and an irrigation system was damaged.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant flash flooding occurred in Eldorado. Ferrell Hospital sustained floodwater damage with 55,000 sq ft of the hospital affected, requiring transfer of patients to other facilities. Flooding on roads leading to the hospital hindered emergency crews. Several streets around the city flooded, and water intruded into the Eldorado High School entrance. Flash flooding transitioned to a flood event through the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State. ||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-1500 J/kg in S Illinois. Effective bulk shear was 40-50 kts with STP of 1. Precipitable water was 1.5-1.75 in. The morning storms mostly missed just south of S Illinois with some street flooding in Johnston City. ||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 2000-3500 J/kg in S Illinois. For S Illinois, effective bulk shear rose to 50 kts, with SRH around 150-250 m2/s2 and STP of 2-3.5. 0-1 km shear was 20 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1200-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds. ||Three tornadoes occurred in S Illinois. Two of these were with the late afternoon to early evening supercells. An EF-3 in the Lake of Egypt area of Johnson County debarked some trees, damaged several homes in the Eagle Point Bay subdivision, and injured one person. An EF-1 tornado in Johnson and Union Counties ahead of the approaching cold front tracked southeastward to Buncombe. The third tornado, an EF-1, was with the cold front in the Grayville area, tracking into Gibson County, IN. The supercells produced several hail reports, with a 2 inch hail report near the Williamson/Saline County border. Training heavy rain produced significant flash flooding for the second time in a month in Saline County, focused on Harrisburg with U.S. 45 flooded and at least one water rescue. Rainfall amounts in Southern Illinois were 1-4 inches, with the highest amounts in Alexander/Pulaski County and along a line from Williamson to Gallatin County. Numerous trees and power lines were damaged with the supercells and the frontal passage. Late evening power outages amounted to roughly a third of Williamson, Union, Johnson, and Alexander Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed trees and power lines occurred as a thunderstorm line moved through with over 1000 without power.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Showers and thunderstorms developed late in the day on Aug 27th in northeast Missouri and spread into southeast Iowa and far western Illinois into the evening. Large hail up to golf ball size fell for nearly one half hour straight in Rutledge, MO.||A pronounced downburst (powerful winds that descend from a thunderstorm and spread out quickly once they hit the ground) caused widespread damage in Burlington, IA, knocking down trees, limbs, and powerlines. Five homes had holes in the roofs due to falling limbs. A commercial storage building had a partial roof collapse and another large tall outbuilding had partial roofing peeled back, consistent with wind estimates of at least 80 mph. ||Rainfall totals of 2 to 4 inches were observed in parts of northeast Missouri and southeast Iowa. ||Oppressive heat and humidity that began on the 26th and persisted into the 27th helped spawn these storms. Both Dubuque (tied) and Moline (exceeded) daily record highs on the 27th, with Moline reaching 98�� and Dubuque 94��.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Downed fencing reported with estimated 60 MPH wind gusts.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State. ||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-1500 J/kg in S Illinois. Effective bulk shear was 40-50 kts with STP of 1. Precipitable water was 1.5-1.75 in. The morning storms mostly missed just south of S Illinois with some street flooding in Johnston City. ||Heading into the evening, MLCAPE values at 1800CST were 2000-3500 J/kg in S Illinois. For S Illinois, effective bulk shear rose to 50 kts, with SRH around 150-250 m2/s2 and STP of 2-3.5. 0-1 km shear was 20 kts. Mid-level lapse rates were 7-7.5 C/km. DCAPE of 1200-1400 J/kg provided ample opportunity for downburst winds. ||Three tornadoes occurred in S Illinois. Two of these were with the late afternoon to early evening supercells. An EF-3 in the Lake of Egypt area of Johnson County debarked some trees, damaged several homes in the Eagle Point Bay subdivision, and injured one person. An EF-1 tornado in Johnson and Union Counties ahead of the approaching cold front tracked southeastward to Buncombe. The third tornado, an EF-1, was with the cold front in the Grayville area, tracking into Gibson County, IN. The supercells produced several hail reports, with a 2 inch hail report near the Williamson/Saline County border. Training heavy rain produced significant flash flooding for the second time in a month in Saline County, focused on Harrisburg with U.S. 45 flooded and at least one water rescue. Rainfall amounts in Southern Illinois were 1-4 inches, with the highest amounts in Alexander/Pulaski County and along a line from Williamson to Gallatin County. Numerous trees and power lines were damaged with the supercells and the frontal passage. Late evening power outages amounted to roughly a third of Williamson, Union, Johnson, and Alexander Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Training thunderstorms produced significant street flooding in Harrisburg. Floodwaters were estimated at 6 to 12 inches deeper than usual for floodprone streets. Water crossed U.S. 45 south of Harrisburg. The fire department was dispatched to a car floating in a Walmart parking lot. Roughly 2-3 inches of rain (radar estimates were 3-4 inches but hail contamination is likely) fell within 2 hours between 1800-2000CST.

Training heavy rain produced significant flash flooding for the second time in a month in Saline County, focused on Harrisburg with U.S. 45 flooded and at least one water rescue. Rainfall amounts in Southern Illinois were 1-4 inches, with the highest amounts in Alexander/Pulaski County and along a line from Williamson to Gallatin County. Numerous trees and power lines were damaged with the supercells and the frontal passage. Late evening power outages amounted to roughly a third of Williamson, Union, Johnson, and Alexander Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees were uprooted in Herrin as a result of thunderstorm downdraft winds.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 27th, a complex of severe thunderstorms impacted northern and northeast Illinois producing widespread wind damage, mostly to trees and power lines, as well as several instances of severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos and videos shared to social media showed rapid retreat of water levels at Foster Beach during ongoing severe thunderstorms.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated late afternoon storms morphed into a fairly small mesoscale convective system that surged southeastward through Southwestern Illinois. MLCAPE was 3000 J/kg, decreasing to 2000 J/kg for the evening. Effective bulk shear was 30 kts. A localized swath of significant wind damage occurred in portions of Southwestern Illinois with the most concentrated area of wind damage near Karnak. Other areas receiving wind damage were Gorham, Dongola, Cypress, and Joppa.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds snapped a tree near the park on the east side of Gorham. Power poles were downed as well.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the evening of the 27th, a complex of severe thunderstorms impacted northern and northeast Illinois producing widespread wind damage, mostly to trees and power lines, as well as several instances of severe hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The NOS gauge at Calumet Harbor recorded water level fluctuations of 1 to 1.5 feet due to thunderstorm activity over the lake. Fluctuations began around 4:30 PM and continued through roughly 9 PM CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the late afternoon of the 30th, a developing line of strong to severe thunderstorms impacted far southern portions of the Chicago WFO CWA producing a few instances of hail and wind damage.
A couple of water rescues were required at Illinois Lake Michigan beaches on the 18th. One 16 year old boy unfortunately succumbed to the dangerous swimming conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two men were transported to an area hospital after requiring rescue from Lake Michigan at Illinois Beach State Park in Zion. A Beach Hazards Statement was in effect at the time advertising 6 to 10 foot waves and dangerous currents along the Illinois lakeshore.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Southern Illinois, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-4 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred on roads in Christopher. Radar estimates were 1-1.5 inches of rain over Christopher between 1430-1515CST which brought the 6 hour rainfall total up to 1.5-2.5 inches.

Flash flooding transitioned to a flood event through the evening.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Numerous thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the afternoon of August 30th. Due to a modest instability - low wind shear environment, these thunderstorms were pulse in nature with wet microbursts being the primary threat. Although an abundance of microbursts occurred, only one was severe in Macoupin, producing tree damage. A landspout also developed along outflow passing through Randolph County, producing minor damage along its path.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds downed trees just northeast of Medora.

The supercells produced several hail reports, with a 2 inch hail report near the Williamson/Saline County border. Training heavy rain produced significant flash flooding for the second time in a month in Saline County, focused on Harrisburg with U.S. 45 flooded and at least one water rescue. Rainfall amounts in Southern Illinois were 1-4 inches, with the highest amounts in Alexander/Pulaski County and along a line from Williamson to Gallatin County. Numerous trees and power lines were damaged with the supercells and the frontal passage. Late evening power outages amounted to roughly a third of Williamson, Union, Johnson, and Alexander Counties.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Powerful straight line winds impacted the Stiritz community during the evening. Most damage was in the form of downed tree limbs, but a few smaller trees were uprooted as well. Several miles east on Paulton Rd, a small, older barn lost most of its roof. Estimated winds were 70 to 90 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The second major severe weather outbreak for the month occurred on the 26th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a shortwave trough centered in the middle of the country with a 60 kt mid-level jet moved across northern Arkansas. A weak surface low was moving into SE Missouri during the morning with a secondary low located further northwest. A warm frontal boundary was draped across our area. A bowing line progressed east across SE Missouri into W Kentucky and far S Illinois between 0500-1000CST, followed by a mostly sub-severe second line that exited the region by 1300CST. The environment recovered producing fuel for more severe storms, starting with supercells 1800-2100CST with a line of storms moving east-southeastward across the entire Quad State after 1900CST, crossing through by midnight. Across the Quad State, this event produced the record for most tornado track miles in a single day in office history, along with the second most warnings issued in a day (just behind the May 8, 2024 event). Widespread straight-line wind and tornado damage resulted in over 100,000 customers without power across the Quad State. ||For the morning, MLCAPE values at 0700CST were 1000-1500 J/kg in S Illinois. EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Discrete supercells developed over southeast Missouri during the afternoon of August 15th. These thunderstorms moved east into Illinois where one cell produced minor wind damage in far southern Randolph County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An isolated storm brought golf ball size hail to portions of Whiteside County Illinois the evening of August 5th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Late in the evening of August 15th, an area of storms moved through southeast Iowa and west central Illinois. These brought large hail and damaging winds to portions of the area, along with heavy rainfall.

A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Southern Illinois, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-4 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A swath of straight line winds of 75 to 85 mph began about 6 miles northwest of Johnsonville, IL and extended east northeast into the town of Rinard. Most of the damage in this swath included uprooted trees, as well as trees or large tree branches snapped. There were some homes that were damaged due to trees falling on them. Several power lines and poles were also broken because of fallen trees. A localized downburst occurred within this area of straight line winds on the south end of Brush Creek Campground, just west northwest of Sam Dale Lake. Winds of around 100 mph caused a small swath of several dozen snapped pine trees. A few buildings on the campground were also damaged. The wind damage swath is estimated at 11.5 miles in length and 1.7 miles in width. Additional wind damage was also reported in other parts of the county with this line of storms, but it was much more sporadic.

On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Southern Illinois, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-4 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail estimated to penny size was reported west of Ava.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced instances of wind damage, mostly to trees, across parts of northeast Illinois during the evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A flag pole snapped and tree damage was observed near Lake Holiday in Somonauk. A video shared also shows an apparent gustnado over Lake Holiday.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A line of thunderstorms produced instances of wind damage, mostly to trees, across parts of northeast Illinois during the evening of the 16th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Photos showed several large tree limbs snapped in Sandwich, one hanging on power lines.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A robust squall line swept through the I-64 corridor of Southern Illinois during the early afternoon hours on the 1st. Numerous wind damage reports were received, primarily in Wayne, Hamilton, and White Counties with many trees and powerlines damaged along with several farm structures. Utility data indicated that roughly half of Wayne County lost power with this storm line. MLCAPE values were approximately 3000 J/kg, DCAPE around 1000 J/kg, and effective bulk shear was 30 kts. Scattered showers and storms continued through the evening and overnight as a cold front moved through during the night, with storm activity in the later convection remaining sub-severe in Southern Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines and a power pole were downed in Rinard as a thunderstorm line surged eastward through northern Wayne County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the daytime on December 14th, widespread freezing rain occurred across eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central and northwest Illinois.|Some of the more notable impacts were; Numerous slick roads in and around the Cedar Rapids, IA metro area where over a half inch of flat ice was observed (CID Airport total of 0.68). Iowa City, IA saw over a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Multiple jackknifed trucks on I-80 as reported through social media. Numerous branches down across the area due to icing. These along with ice on wires caused power outages, with some counties in southeast Iowa temporarily having 30% of customers without power.|McDonough County in west central Illinois, including the Macomb area, where icing over 0.25 resulted in several large branches down due to ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and DOT reported widespread slick conditions with a 0.2 to 0.3 of an inch of ice accumulation on the ground and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system positioned north of the Great Lakes resulted in a cold front sweeping across Illinois on the evening of December 4th. Breezy northwest winds occurred across central Illinois, with measured gusts as high as 55 to 60 mph. These high winds produced isolated damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Wind gust measured at Vermilion Regional Airport (KDNV).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A weak clipper system caused a brief period of light snow during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 2nd. Temperatures remained below freezing, and continued to drop into the 20s. While snow accumulations between a coating to one inch were confined to mainly grassy and elevated surfaces, slick road conditions eventually developed in time for the evening commute that caused accidents across the region. Ice covered bridges and areas of black ice continued through the evening hours after snow had ended. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A fast-moving clipper system moved from west to east across central Illinois during the morning and early afternoon on December 2nd, producing light snow accumulations of a dusting to 1.5 inches. This resulted in slippery roads and numerous vehicle accidents. Most of the accidents were minor, but a few injuries were noted.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow accumulations resulted in slippery roads, and at least five vehicle accidents were reported across Coles County. One accident resulted in injuries, but a specific number of injuries was not provided.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Light snow accumulations resulted in slippery roads, and at least 25 vehicle accidents were reported across Champaign County. Most of the accidents were minor, but a few resulted in injuries. An exact number of injuries was not provided, but at least three people were transported to the hospital.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A narrow, slow-moving band of snow developed during the day on December 12th, resulting in accumulating snow primarily north of I-72. The heaviest totals of 2 to 3.5 occurred in an east-west band extending from Fulton County to northern Vermilion County. The snow resulted in slippery roads, and at least 17 vehicle accidents were reported across eastern Illinois, mainly on I-57, I-72, and I-74.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snow accumulations of one to three inches resulted in slippery roadways, and some vehicle accidents were reported. Most of the accidents were minor, but one injury occurred.

Ahead of an approaching low pressure system, rain overspread central Illinois during the morning hours of December 14th. Temperatures across west-central Illinois were just below freezing, resulting in freezing rain accreting on surfaces. Temperatures warmed above freezing from south to north during the late morning and early afternoon, transitioning precipitation to rain. Total rainfall amounts of up to 1.25 were reported across west-central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Social media report of a tenth of an inch of ice accretion as of 0830 CST.

Ice covered bridges and areas of black ice continued through the evening hours after snow had ended. Key Impacts: transportation delays, traffic accidents.

Ahead of an approaching low pressure system, rain overspread central Illinois during the morning hours of December 14th. Temperatures across west-central Illinois were just below freezing, resulting in freezing rain accreting on surfaces. Temperatures warmed above freezing from south to north during the late morning and early afternoon, transitioning precipitation to rain. Total rainfall amounts of up to 1.25 were reported across west-central Illinois.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tenth of an inch of ice was measured in Beardstown.

A robust dry cold frontal passage brought gusty post-frontal winds through the Evansville Tri-State area overnight on the 4th into the 5th. Winds gusted to around 40 mph near the Wabash River, with gusts of 44mph at Flora (KFOA), 39mph at Olney (KOLY), and 43mph at Vincennes (KLWV).

A rapidly developing low pressure system moved northeastward through the Quad State region from the evening of the 28th through the morning of the 29th. Minor flooding was observed in Hamilton County as a result of rainfall around an inch and a half falling on fairly saturated soils.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water covered portions of Norris City Road and Army Navy Store Road. Flooding was observed with the second round of precipitation for the day, occurring in the early morning.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A storm system that moved across the upper Great Lakes provided the region with strong winds during the late afternoon and evening of the 4th. The winds managed to damage the roof of a building in Chicago, knock down power lines near Gibson city, and damage a number of trees around the area. A handful of reports were received of 50 to 60 mph gusts. A Wind Advisory was in effect at the time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roof damage was noted to a building in Chicago's Douglas neighborhood with considerable debris scattered downwind of the building.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Following a windy day, scattered bands of light to moderate snow, made worse by the breezy conditions, moved across the area during the evening. One band was particularly intense producing locally heavy snow with less than 1/2 mile visibility as it worked across DuPage and southern Cook Counties before moving into northwest Indiana. Snow rapidly accumulated on roadways as a result. A snow squall warning was issued for this band.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Emergency management reported visibility under 500 feet within falling snow for about 20 minutes, followed by completely snow-covered roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong low pressure system positioned north of the Great Lakes resulted in a cold front sweeping across Illinois on the evening of December 4th. Breezy northwest winds occurred across central Illinois, with measured gusts as high as 55 to 60 mph. These high winds produced isolated damage to power lines.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Power lines blown down on both Prospect Road and Laramie Street in Peoria.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the mid-afternoon into the early evening on December 4, a powerful cold front stretching from a weather system in the northern Great Lakes region swept through eastern Iowa and northwest Illinois. This front ushered in much colder air along with very strong gradient winds. None of the wind gusts were associated with any thunderstorms. Several wind gusts over 58 mph were recorded across the region, and several semi trailers were blown over in northeast Iowa near Dubuque.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Measured by the Moline Airport weather equipement (ASOS).

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: During the daytime on December 14th, widespread freezing rain occurred across eastern Iowa, northeast Missouri, and west central and northwest Illinois.|Some of the more notable impacts were; Numerous slick roads in and around the Cedar Rapids, IA metro area where over a half inch of flat ice was observed (CID Airport total of 0.68). Iowa City, IA saw over a quarter inch of ice accumulation. Multiple jackknifed trucks on I-80 as reported through social media. Numerous branches down across the area due to icing. These along with ice on wires caused power outages, with some counties in southeast Iowa temporarily having 30% of customers without power.|McDonough County in west central Illinois, including the Macomb area, where icing over 0.25 resulted in several large branches down due to ice.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trained spotters and DOT reported widespread slick conditions with a 0.2 to 0.4 of an inch of ice accumulation on the ground and elevated surfaces.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall in early May, culminating in the May 8th severe weather and flooding event, resulted in rising river levels on the Lower Ohio, Big Muddy, and Mississippi Rivers, with Thebes returning to minor flood and Murphysboro, Olmsted, and Cairo reaching flood stage shortly after. Minor flooding in Southern Illinois around these river sites lasted 5 to 8 days with impacts limited to low-lying areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding occurred on the Mississippi River at Thebes in low-lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major outbreak of severe weather occurred on the 8th for the Quad State region. On the synoptic scale, a longwave trough was centered across the Rockies with deep-layer southwesterly flow from the Southern Plains to the Ohio Valley. A 110 kt upper jet extended from northern KS to IA while a 60 kt mid-level jet was positioned across northern MZ into IA. Surface low pressure near the MZ/KS border lifted to St. Louis around 12am CST on the 9th. A warm frontal boundary stretched across the Quad State, with a cold front extending southward from the surface low. ||The environment became unstable by early afternoon, especially in far Southern Illinois, with MLCAPE of 2000-3000 J/kg. Deep-layer shear was on the order of 55-65 kts, though low-level shear was 15 kts, increasing to 20 kts later in the day. SRH was around 100-150 m2/s2 with higher values north towards St. Louis. STP values were 2-4 due to deep-layer shear and instability. Supercell activity was more prominent during the afternoon in Southern Illinois with lapse rates of 6.5-7 C/km aiding with hail production.||For Southern Illinois, storms moved through with the cold front late evening on the 7th through the overnight hours with tree and power line damage in Joppa. The front reversed and lifted north during the morning, with storms entering Southwest Illinois late morning. A brief EF-1 tornado in Cora damaged trees and a few homes while injuring one person due to a falling tree limb. The afternoon brought a significant ramping up of supercell development and the arrival of a line of storms from the west. An EF-0 tornado crossed from Jackson County into Williamson County. An EF-0 tornado went through central subdivisions of the Lake of Egypt community, while an EF-1 tornado progressed eastward in Saline County with a broader area of straight-line wind damage around it. An initial evening round of storms swept through the northern half of Southern Illinois with wind damage most prominent in northern Wayne County with a swath of 75 to 85 mph straight-line wind damage and an embedded downburst.||Training heavy rainfall during the afternoon produced flash flooding from Jackson County eastward with rainfall totals commonly in the 2.5-4 inch range for northern Jackson, Williamson, Saline, Gallatin, and northern Union County. Hardest hit by flooding were Eldorado and Harrisburg in Saline County which received 1.5-2 inches within an hour. In Eldorado, Ferrell Hospital had floodwaters intrude on 55,000 sq ft of hospital space, necessitating transfer of patients. The high school had water in the entrance areas, and numerous roads flooded. Harrisburg avoided some of the higher end impacts of Eldorado but had many roads flooded for much of the afternoon and evening.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado (max of EF-0 in Jackson County) began southeast of Carbondale at Giant City Rd. Several large tree limbs were downed on Wagon Wheel Road and County Line Rd as the tornado crossed into Williamson County. Peak winds for the Jackson County portion of this tornado were estimated at 80 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of strong thunderstorms south of the I-72 corridor during the late afternoon and early evening of September 20th.  One of the cells produced nickel-sized hail in Cumberland County.

Severe drought conditions carried into November from an abnormally dry October. However, frequent bouts of rain improved drought conditions throughout the first few weeks of November. O'Hare Airport ended the month with 3.63 of rain, 1.2 more than its monthly average of 2.42. The Rockford Airport fell only about 0.2 shy of its monthly normal with 2.04.

Light to moderate snow overspread the area during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 30th, as a clipper system moved through. A localized band of heavier snow developed that evening along Interstate 64, causing reduced visibility below 1 mile. Snowfall rates between 0.5 to 1.0 inch per hour had occurred into the night before tapering off to lingering flurries around Midnight, causing snow accumulations between 2 to 4 inches. Outside the snow band, a sharp cut off to the south resulted only in 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulation. Snow initially was confined to grassy and elevated surfaces, but a combination of nightfall and heavier intensity eventually overcame warm pavement temperatures. Temperatures initially near 32 to 34 degrees fell into the 20s that night. This led to icy and slick roadway conditions on untreated surfaces. Key Impacts: transportation delays.

The remnants of Hurricane Helene lifted slowly northwestward across Kentucky during the afternoon of September 27th...with a 988mb low analyzed near Louisville, Kentucky by early evening.  As the pressure gradient tightened, very strong winds developed across central Illinois.  The strongest wind gusts of 55-65mph were concentrated along and southeast of a Champaign to Decatur line, where scattered minor wind damage occurred.  A peak wind gust of 66mph was measured at the Coles County Memorial Airport between Mattoon and Charleston.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A peak wind gust of 59mph was measured by a handheld anemometer in Casey.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Severe drought conditions carried into November from an abnormally dry October. However, frequent bouts of rain improved drought conditions throughout the first few weeks of November. O'Hare Airport ended the month with 3.63 of rain, 1.2 more than its monthly average of 2.42. The Rockford Airport fell only about 0.2 shy of its monthly normal with 2.04.

Light to moderate snow overspread the area during the late morning and afternoon hours on the 30th, as a clipper system moved through. A localized band of heavier snow developed that evening along Interstate 64, causing reduced visibility below 1 mile. Snowfall rates between 0.5 to 1.0 inch per hour had occurred into the night before tapering off to lingering flurries around Midnight, causing snow accumulations between 2 to 4 inches. Outside the snow band, a sharp cut off to the south resulted only in 1 to 2 inches of snow accumulation. Snow initially was confined to grassy and elevated surfaces, but a combination of nightfall and heavier intensity eventually overcame warm pavement temperatures. Temperatures initially near 32 to 34 degrees fell into the 20s that night. This led to icy and slick roadway conditions on untreated surfaces. Key Impacts: transportation delays.

A slow-moving frontal boundary brought several rounds of showers and thunderstorms to central Illinois from November 3rd through 5th.  Storm total rainfall during that time ranged from 2 to 4 inches, with a few locations across the Illinois River Valley picking up greater than 4 inches.  Due to antecedent drought conditions through September and October, much of the heavy rainfall readily soaked into the ground and caused little issue other than standing water in urban locations.  Localized flash flooding occurred under a viaduct in Champaign likely due to storm drains clogged with leaves and other debris.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A vehicle stalled in high water beneath a viaduct in Champaign.
