EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms initiated across central and northern Iowa during the afternoon of July 14th.  A highly unstable and sheared environment quickly led to upscale growth into a Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) that tracked east-southeastward along the northern periphery of a prominent upper-level ridge centered over the southern Plains into the lower Mississippi River Valley.  Widespread wind damage occurred within the path of the MCS during the evening of July 14th into the pre-dawn hours of July 15th...mainly north of the I-74 corridor.  National Weather Service Lincoln damage survey teams found 3 tornado touchdowns along the leading edge of the storms in Marshall County, all 3 of which were rated EF-1. Additional analysis of Sentinel satellite data found two more brief tornadoes occurred in rural Marshall County. These two were rated EF-Unknown.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Mesoscale Convective System (MCS) that formed over eastern Nebraska and western Iowa during the pre-dawn hours of July 30th decayed as it tracked southeastward toward the Mississippi River.  As it moved further southeastward, it re-intensified across south-central Illinois during peak heating.  The newly formed cluster of storms produced scattered wind damage and a few hailstones as large as quarters along and south of a Lincoln...to Mattoon...to Lawrenceville line during the afternoon of July 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front interacting with a seasonably warm and moist environment triggered clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms across central Illinois during the afternoon and evening of August 30th.  One cell dropped nickel-sized hail, while others produced wind gusts of around 60mph and minor wind damage.  In addition, urban flash flooding occurred across portions of Springfield and Champaign.  No road closures or water rescues were reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several roads were flooded in Springfield, including the 1600 block of Sangamon Avenue and parts of Clock Tower Drive and Durkin Drive.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large upper-level ridge of high pressure centered over the Midwest brought very hot weather to central Illinois on August 26th and 27th.  High temperatures were mostly in the lower to middle 90s on August 26th, but climbed into the 95-100 degree range on the 27th.  With dewpoints hovering in the upper 70s to near 80 degrees, heat index readings peaked from 105 to 115 degrees along and north of the I-72 corridor during the afternoon of August 27th.  While no heat-related deaths were reported, this was the hottest period of weather across central Illinois during the entire summer of 2024.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heat index readings peaked at 110-115 degrees during the afternoon of August 27th.  No heat-related deaths were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms across north-central Illinois during the evening of June 13th into the pre-dawn hours of June 14th.  Most of the activity was focused along and north of a Beardstown to Champaign line.  Numerous trees and power lines were blown down thanks to 60-70 mph wind gusts, while a few reports of hail as large as quarters were also received.  The most significant wind damage occurred across northeastern McLean County from Chenoa and Lexington southeastward through Cropsey and Anchor where a damage survey team determined widespread wind gusts of 80-110 mph as well as 5 tornado touchdowns.  A total of 33 high-tension electrical transmission towers were severely damaged as a result of the powerful winds across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near the intersection of North 3050 East Road and East 2700 North Road at 8:29 PM CDT.  The tornado tracked eastward and destroyed a barn before quickly dissipating less than 1 mile from its initial touchdown at 8:30 PM CDT.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms tracked across Wisconsin and Michigan during the pre-dawn hours of June 25th...triggering an additional cluster of storms further southwest in the Chicago metropolitan area.  As these storms dropped southeastward, one of the cells grazed Vermilion County...dropping quarter-sized hail near Hoopeston shortly after sunrise.  By mid-morning, a line of thunderstorms had developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the Wisconsin-Michigan complex from central Iowa eastward to southern Lake Michigan.  As the line sagged southward, it produced damaging wind gusts of around 60 mph and sporadic hail as large as quarters as far south as the I-72 corridor.  One of the cells along the line became particularly intense by early afternoon and produced significant crop damage east and southeast of Paris in Edgar County due to wind-driven large hail.  The largest stones were golf ball-sized just north of Dennison.  A wake low generated by the cell produced 60-70 mph wind gusts and additional wind damage across portions of Champaign and Piatt counties after the storm had passed.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on the 12th in the Quad State. Wind damage occurred in the town of Sesser, including tree and powerline damage. Storms from the previous evening in Sesser might have made some trees more susceptible to damage. Meanwhile, torrential rainfall with nearly stationary storms near Tamaroa produced flash flooding. Isolated storms continued overnight as heavy rain and minimal storm motion caused flash flooding issues in Murphysboro and other locations in or near Jackson County, Illinois. Storm total rainfall peaked around 2-3 inches in parts of Jackson and Perry Counties, most of which fell within an hour or two at each respective location. Shear was minimal while SBCAPE reached 2000-3000 J/kg during the afternoon on the 12th, dropping to 1000-2000 J/kg overnight. PW values were near 1.75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue occurred at the Union Pacific Railroad underpass on Business Hwy 13 with water halfway up the door. Another vehicle was stalled in high water at 14th and Maple Streets in Murphysboro. Water came into the door of a residence in the 1200 block of N 14th St. Radar estimated 3-hr rainfall totals were 2-3.5 inches most of which fell between 215-330CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A particularly explosive storm environment developed on the afternoon of the 30th. MLCAPE values rose to 3500-4000 J/kg, 0-3 km CAPE of 100-150 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40 kts, and 0-3 km shear of 20-30 kts. Temperatures rose to the low 90s with dew points in the upper 70s to near 80, yielding heat index values near 110. For Southern Illinois, an MCS dove southeastward during the afternoon, producing sporadic thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds blew a tree down on power lines. Multiple trees were downed onto state roads and a utility pole was blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With breezy southerly winds bringing dew points in the mid to upper 70s to Southern Illinois under high pressure, heat indices in most of Southern Illinois surged to 105-113F on the 14th and 15th. The highest observed heat index was 113F at the Mount Vernon Airport AWOS on the 15th.

A cluster of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the pre-dawn hours, and eventually formed a small bowing segment as they progressed across central Illinois during the morning. These storms produced isolated instances of wind damage, as well as a brief EF-1 tornado in Vermilion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF-1 tornado touched down about 3.5 miles south of East Lynn along E 3800 North Road, where it completely destroyed seven grain bins and tossed the debris for about a half mile to the east-northeast. A couple power poles were snapped at that location as well. The tornado moved to the east-northeast as it damaged trees, home siding, and the roof of an outbuilding along N 770 East Road. The tornado then progressed in a more northerly direction as it damaged some trees at a residence about a half mile to the north, before dissipating in the field to the northeast after being on the ground for a total of two minutes.

Numerous trees and power lines were blown down thanks to 60-70 mph wind gusts, while a few reports of hail as large as quarters were also received.  The most significant wind damage occurred across northeastern McLean County from Chenoa and Lexington southeastward through Cropsey and Anchor where a damage survey team determined widespread wind gusts of 80-110 mph as well as 5 tornado touchdowns.  A total of 33 high-tension electrical transmission towers were severely damaged as a result of the powerful winds across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree was blown down onto a house.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on the 12th in the Quad State. Wind damage occurred in the town of Sesser, including tree and powerline damage. Storms from the previous evening in Sesser might have made some trees more susceptible to damage. Meanwhile, torrential rainfall with nearly stationary storms near Tamaroa produced flash flooding. Isolated storms continued overnight as heavy rain and minimal storm motion caused flash flooding issues in Murphysboro and other locations in or near Jackson County, Illinois. Storm total rainfall peaked around 2-3 inches in parts of Jackson and Perry Counties, most of which fell within an hour or two at each respective location. Shear was minimal while SBCAPE reached 2000-3000 J/kg during the afternoon on the 12th, dropping to 1000-2000 J/kg overnight. PW values were near 1.75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roads in northeast Jackson County were flooded by heavy rains. Field flooding lingered with drainage ditches swollen and flowing. Radar estimated 3-hour rainfall totals were 2 to 4 inches with the highest amounts falling just northeast of Vergennes between 300-500CST.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme rainfall totals occurred during the morning of the 16th in and around Nashville, Illinois, just northwest of the Quad State region, which resulted in a secondary dam failure. Neighboring portions of Jefferson and Perry Counties received 3-6 and 2-5 inches, respectively. Extensive street flooding occurred in Mount Vernon, while some roads north of Tamaroa flooded for the second time within a week. Through the day, flash flooding transitioned to standing water and rising creek levels. Rayse Creek, Beaucoup Creek, and the Little Muddy River rapidly rose and flooded, with a water rescue at a Beaucoup Creek tributary. Rayse Creek at Waltonville reached 17.46 ft, just over 3.5 ft above the previous record. Significant creek flooding continued into the following day. PW values were 2.25 inches in the hardest hit areas with significant moisture transport at 850 mb. A slow-moving west-east boundary was located just to the north, channeling heavy rainfall eastward on the south side.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPING report was submitted for minor creek and field flooding in northwest Wayne County. Radar rainfall estimates were 1-2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Elevated water levels flowing down the Mississippi River from locations north of the Quad State resulted in minor flooding on the Mississippi in Southwest Illinois from early to mid July. Impacts were mostly limited to low-lying agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding began across from Cape Girardeau in early July and continued through the third week of July with impacts mainly limited to low-lying agricultural areas. The river crested just below moderate flood stage on July 11th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dove southeastward through Southern Illinois during the late evening hours on the 29th. A few of these storms produced damaging wind gusts, downing trees and powerlines. MLCAPE fell from 3000 to 1500 J/kg over the course of the evening, while DCAPE remained around 1200 J/kg. Effective bulk shear was approximately 35 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measured to nickel size fell on the southwest side of Mount Vernon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A mesoscale convective system surged through the Quad State during the overnight hours early on the 5th. Significant wind damage was observed in Murphysboro and Cambria from a surge in the storm line. Numerous trees and powerlines were downed in Murphysboro and approximately 40 trees were downed in Cambria. MLCAPE in Southwestern Illinois was 1000-2000 J/kg while effective bulk shear was 30 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Approximately 40 trees were downed in and around Cambria as a surging storm line moved northeastward. One tree fell onto a manufactured home.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A particularly explosive storm environment developed on the afternoon of the 30th. MLCAPE values rose to 3500-4000 J/kg, 0-3 km CAPE of 100-150 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40 kts, and 0-3 km shear of 20-30 kts. Temperatures rose to the low 90s with dew points in the upper 70s to near 80, yielding heat index values near 110. For Southern Illinois, an MCS dove southeastward during the afternoon, producing sporadic thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tree fell through a garage heavily damaging a car on the northeast side of Mount Carmel.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105-114F were observed in much of Southern Illinois on the 3rd and 4th while the I-64 corridor only reached heat advisory criteria on the 4th. Late afternoon thunderstorms in Southern Illinois on the 3rd lowered heat indices for the evening while thunderstorms moved through Southern Illinois mid-morning on the 4th, temporarily delaying daytime heating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms across north-central Illinois during the evening of June 13th into the pre-dawn hours of June 14th.  Most of the activity was focused along and north of a Beardstown to Champaign line.  Numerous trees and power lines were blown down thanks to 60-70 mph wind gusts, while a few reports of hail as large as quarters were also received.  The most significant wind damage occurred across northeastern McLean County from Chenoa and Lexington southeastward through Cropsey and Anchor where a damage survey team determined widespread wind gusts of 80-110 mph as well as 5 tornado touchdowns.  A total of 33 high-tension electrical transmission towers were severely damaged as a result of the powerful winds across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous trees and powerlines were blown down.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms tracked across Wisconsin and Michigan during the pre-dawn hours of June 25th...triggering an additional cluster of storms further southwest in the Chicago metropolitan area.  As these storms dropped southeastward, one of the cells grazed Vermilion County...dropping quarter-sized hail near Hoopeston shortly after sunrise.  By mid-morning, a line of thunderstorms had developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the Wisconsin-Michigan complex from central Iowa eastward to southern Lake Michigan.  As the line sagged southward, it produced damaging wind gusts of around 60 mph and sporadic hail as large as quarters as far south as the I-72 corridor.  One of the cells along the line became particularly intense by early afternoon and produced significant crop damage east and southeast of Paris in Edgar County due to wind-driven large hail.  The largest stones were golf ball-sized just north of Dennison.  A wake low generated by the cell produced 60-70 mph wind gusts and additional wind damage across portions of Champaign and Piatt counties after the storm had passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A few tree branches were snapped.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme rainfall totals occurred during the morning of the 16th in and around Nashville, Illinois, just northwest of the Quad State region, which resulted in a secondary dam failure. Neighboring portions of Jefferson and Perry Counties received 3-6 and 2-5 inches, respectively. Extensive street flooding occurred in Mount Vernon, while some roads north of Tamaroa flooded for the second time within a week. Through the day, flash flooding transitioned to standing water and rising creek levels. Rayse Creek, Beaucoup Creek, and the Little Muddy River rapidly rose and flooded, with a water rescue at a Beaucoup Creek tributary. Rayse Creek at Waltonville reached 17.46 ft, just over 3.5 ft above the previous record. Significant creek flooding continued into the following day. PW values were 2.25 inches in the hardest hit areas with significant moisture transport at 850 mb. A slow-moving west-east boundary was located just to the north, channeling heavy rainfall eastward on the south side.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Extensive street flooding occurred in Mount Vernon. IL-15 became impassable due to high water just east of Woodlawn. Radar estimated rainfall totals in Mount Vernon were 2.5-4 inches, while totals in western portions of Jefferson County reached 5-7 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall in Southwest Illinois on July 16th resulted in water levels at Thebes briefly returning to minor flood, with limited impacts to low-lying agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Torrential rainfall in portions of Southwestern Illinois resulted in a secondary surge of water levels on the Mississippi River, briefly returning gage levels at Thebes to minor flood.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105-114F were observed in much of Southern Illinois on the 3rd and 4th while the I-64 corridor only reached heat advisory criteria on the 4th. Strong moisture advection raised dew points to the mid to upper 70s both days. The highest observed heat index was 114F on the 4th at the Cairo Regional Airport AWOS. Late afternoon thunderstorms in Southern Illinois on the 3rd lowered heat indices for the evening while thunderstorms moved through Southern Illinois mid-morning on the 4th, temporarily delaying daytime heating.

Elevated water levels flowing down the Mississippi River from locations north of the Quad State resulted in minor flooding on the Mississippi in Southwest Illinois from early to mid July. Impacts were mostly limited to low-lying agricultural areas.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minor flooding began near Thebes in early July and continued through mid July with impacts mainly limited to low-lying agricultural areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms dove southeastward through Southern Illinois during the late evening hours on the 29th. A few of these storms produced damaging wind gusts, downing trees and powerlines. MLCAPE fell from 3000 to 1500 J/kg over the course of the evening, while DCAPE remained around 1200 J/kg. Effective bulk shear was approximately 35 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Trees and power lines were downed as a thunderstorm moved southeastward across Johnston City. A portable carport was lifted up and wrapped around a garage. One house had some soffit damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training heavy rainfall developed in portions of Southern Illinois during the evening hours on the 28th, with flash flooding developing in Wayne and Saline Counties. Eldorado was hit hard for a second time with rainfall totals around 5 inches, prompting water rescues. Water intruded into buildings including the Ferrell Hospital, which had already been heavily impacted from a flood in May. PW values were up to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny size hail was measured from a supercell thunderstorm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A particularly explosive storm environment developed on the afternoon of the 30th. MLCAPE values rose to 3500-4000 J/kg, 0-3 km CAPE of 100-150 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40 kts, and 0-3 km shear of 20-30 kts. Temperatures rose to the low 90s with dew points in the upper 70s to near 80, yielding heat index values near 110. For Southern Illinois, an MCS dove southeastward during the afternoon, producing sporadic thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The thunderstorm wind speed was estimated by a trained spotter as the storm tracked eastward across northern Franklin County.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With breezy southerly winds bringing dew points in the mid to upper 70s to Southern Illinois under high pressure, heat indices in most of Southern Illinois surged to 105-113F on the 14th and 15th. The highest observed heat index was 113F at the Mount Vernon Airport AWOS on the 15th.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A funnel cloud was reported nearly to the ground around Hwy 148 and Crocker Rd.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on the 12th in the Quad State. Wind damage occurred in the town of Sesser, including tree and powerline damage. Storms from the previous evening in Sesser might have made some trees more susceptible to damage. Meanwhile, torrential rainfall with nearly stationary storms near Tamaroa produced flash flooding. Isolated storms continued overnight as heavy rain and minimal storm motion caused flash flooding issues in Murphysboro and other locations in or near Jackson County, Illinois. Storm total rainfall peaked around 2-3 inches in parts of Jackson and Perry Counties, most of which fell within an hour or two at each respective location. Shear was minimal while SBCAPE reached 2000-3000 J/kg during the afternoon on the 12th, dropping to 1000-2000 J/kg overnight. PW values were near 1.75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A metal roof was removed and structural damage occurred on Lucretia St in Sesser. Tree and power line damage occurred in multiple spots in Sesser. Storms from the previous evening may have made trees and power lines more susceptible to damage from this storm.

By mid-morning, a line of thunderstorms had developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the Wisconsin-Michigan complex from central Iowa eastward to southern Lake Michigan.  As the line sagged southward, it produced damaging wind gusts of around 60 mph and sporadic hail as large as quarters as far south as the I-72 corridor.  One of the cells along the line became particularly intense by early afternoon and produced significant crop damage east and southeast of Paris in Edgar County due to wind-driven large hail.  The largest stones were golf ball-sized just north of Dennison.  A wake low generated by the cell produced 60-70 mph wind gusts and additional wind damage across portions of Champaign and Piatt counties after the storm had passed.

A cold front triggered clusters of strong to severe thunderstorms across north-central Illinois during the evening of June 13th into the pre-dawn hours of June 14th.  Most of the activity was focused along and north of a Beardstown to Champaign line.  Numerous trees and power lines were blown down thanks to 60-70 mph wind gusts, while a few reports of hail as large as quarters were also received.  The most significant wind damage occurred across northeastern McLean County from Chenoa and Lexington southeastward through Cropsey and Anchor where a damage survey team determined widespread wind gusts of 80-110 mph as well as 5 tornado touchdowns.  A total of 33 high-tension electrical transmission towers were severely damaged as a result of the powerful winds across this area.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A barn door was blown off along Route 47 northwest of Fisher.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Extreme rainfall totals occurred during the morning of the 16th in and around Nashville, Illinois, just northwest of the Quad State region, which resulted in a secondary dam failure. Neighboring portions of Jefferson and Perry Counties received 3-6 and 2-5 inches, respectively. Extensive street flooding occurred in Mount Vernon, while some roads north of Tamaroa flooded for the second time within a week. Through the day, flash flooding transitioned to standing water and rising creek levels. Rayse Creek, Beaucoup Creek, and the Little Muddy River rapidly rose and flooded, with a water rescue at a Beaucoup Creek tributary. Rayse Creek at Waltonville reached 17.46 ft, just over 3.5 ft above the previous record. Significant creek flooding continued into the following day. PW values were 2.25 inches in the hardest hit areas with significant moisture transport at 850 mb. A slow-moving west-east boundary was located just to the north, channeling heavy rainfall eastward on the south side.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A water rescue occurred along a Beaucoup Creek tributary. Significant creek rises occurred due to water draining from torrential rainfall up north.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105-114F were observed in much of Southern Illinois on the 3rd and 4th while the I-64 corridor only reached heat advisory criteria on the 4th. Strong moisture advection raised dew points to the mid to upper 70s both days. The highest observed heat index was 114F on the 4th at the Cairo Regional Airport AWOS. Late afternoon thunderstorms in Southern Illinois on the 3rd lowered heat indices for the evening while thunderstorms moved through Southern Illinois mid-morning on the 4th, temporarily delaying daytime heating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cold front triggered a broken line of thunderstorms across north-central Illinois during the evening of June 25th.  Some of the cells produced wind gusts of around 60 mph, with one storm dropping golf ball-sized hail in Altona in Knox County.  Excessive rainfall rates from another storm created localized flash flooding in Canton in Fulton County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several tree limbs were blown down across a road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of Southern Illinois reached heat indices of 104-109F on the 29th, with more widespread heat indices of 105-116F on the 30th and 31st, extending into the start of August. Dew points reached the upper 70s on the 30th and 31st. The highest heat index reached in Southern Illinois was 116F at the Dixon Springs RAWS site on the 30th. Mid to late afternoon severe thunderstorms interrupted the heat on the 30th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Training heavy rainfall developed in portions of Southern Illinois during the evening hours on the 28th, with flash flooding developing in Wayne and Saline Counties. Eldorado was hit hard for a second time with rainfall totals around 5 inches, prompting water rescues. Water intruded into buildings including the Ferrell Hospital, which had already been heavily impacted from a flood in May. PW values were up to 2 inches.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Roadway flooding occurred on the north side of Fairfield with 4 inches of water flowing across the road. Radar estimated rainfall totals during the late afternoon to early evening were 1.5-2.5 inches near town and 2.5-3.5 inches west of town.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105-114F were observed in much of Southern Illinois on the 3rd and 4th while the I-64 corridor only reached heat advisory criteria on the 4th. Strong moisture advection raised dew points to the mid to upper 70s both days. The highest observed heat index was 114F on the 4th at the Cairo Regional Airport AWOS. Late afternoon thunderstorms in Southern Illinois on the 3rd lowered heat indices for the evening while thunderstorms moved through Southern Illinois mid-morning on the 4th, temporarily delaying daytime heating.

A particularly explosive storm environment developed on the afternoon of the 30th. MLCAPE values rose to 3500-4000 J/kg, 0-3 km CAPE of 100-150 J/kg, effective bulk shear of 30-40 kts, and 0-3 km shear of 20-30 kts. Temperatures rose to the low 90s with dew points in the upper 70s to near 80, yielding heat index values near 110. For Southern Illinois, an MCS dove southeastward during the afternoon, producing sporadic thunderstorm wind damage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The wind gust was measured as a thunderstorm line moved northeastward through Harrisburg. A tree was blown down onto IL-34 between Harrisburg and Raleigh.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Isolated thunderstorms developed during the afternoon hours on the 12th in the Quad State. Wind damage occurred in the town of Sesser, including tree and powerline damage. Storms from the previous evening in Sesser might have made some trees more susceptible to damage. Meanwhile, torrential rainfall with nearly stationary storms near Tamaroa produced flash flooding. Isolated storms continued overnight as heavy rain and minimal storm motion caused flash flooding issues in Murphysboro and other locations in or near Jackson County, Illinois. Storm total rainfall peaked around 2-3 inches in parts of Jackson and Perry Counties, most of which fell within an hour or two at each respective location. Shear was minimal while SBCAPE reached 2000-3000 J/kg during the afternoon on the 12th, dropping to 1000-2000 J/kg overnight. PW values were near 1.75 inch.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Road flooding was reported in northeastern portions of Perry County, including US-51 north of Tamaroa. Radar estimated rainfall totals between 1445-1615CST were around 3 inches just north of Tamaroa and 1.5-2 inches in Tamaroa.

A mesoscale convective system surged through the Quad State during the overnight hours early on the 5th. Significant wind damage was observed in Murphysboro and Cambria from a surge in the storm line. Numerous trees and powerlines were downed in Murphysboro and approximately 40 trees were downed in Cambria. MLCAPE in Southwestern Illinois was 1000-2000 J/kg while effective bulk shear was 30 kts.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Numerous mature trees and powerlines were downed in and around Murphysboro as a surging segment of a thunderstorm line moved northeastward through the area. Several trees fell across roads in Jackson County including in Gorham.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large complex of thunderstorms tracked across Wisconsin and Michigan during the pre-dawn hours of June 25th...triggering an additional cluster of storms further southwest in the Chicago metropolitan area.  As these storms dropped southeastward, one of the cells grazed Vermilion County...dropping quarter-sized hail near Hoopeston shortly after sunrise.  By mid-morning, a line of thunderstorms had developed along the outflow boundary left behind by the Wisconsin-Michigan complex from central Iowa eastward to southern Lake Michigan.  As the line sagged southward, it produced damaging wind gusts of around 60 mph and sporadic hail as large as quarters as far south as the I-72 corridor.  One of the cells along the line became particularly intense by early afternoon and produced significant crop damage east and southeast of Paris in Edgar County due to wind-driven large hail.  The largest stones were golf ball-sized just north of Dennison.  A wake low generated by the cell produced 60-70 mph wind gusts and additional wind damage across portions of Champaign and Piatt counties after the storm had passed.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Several trees were blown down across Curtis Road west of Prospect Avenue in Savoy from a wake low passage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms developed during the late-afternoon to evening hours in Southern Illinois. These storms were generally sub-severe but one particularly strong storm produced widespread tree and powerline damage in Sesser and Benton in Franklin County. MLCAPE was 1000-1500 J/kg while effective bulk shear was low.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An mPING report was submitted for a tree uprooted in Grayville.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Heat indices of 105-114F were observed in much of Southern Illinois on the 3rd and 4th while the I-64 corridor only reached heat advisory criteria on the 4th. Strong moisture advection raised dew points to the mid to upper 70s both days. The highest observed heat index was 114F on the 4th at the Cairo Regional Airport AWOS. Late afternoon thunderstorms in Southern Illinois on the 3rd lowered heat indices for the evening while thunderstorms moved through Southern Illinois mid-morning on the 4th, temporarily delaying daytime heating.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A cluster of thunderstorms developed ahead of a cold front during the pre-dawn hours, and eventually formed a small bowing segment as they progressed across central Illinois during the morning. These storms produced isolated instances of wind damage, as well as a brief EF-1 tornado in Vermilion County.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple tree limbs were blown down, some of which were up to four inches in diameter.

The remnants of Hurricane Beryl tracked north-northeastward to the Quad State region on the 9th, with the center of circulation following near the Lower Ohio River. The bulk of the stratiform precipitation was northwest of the Quad State, keeping flooding issues from developing. In Southern Illinois, the one report of damaging wind came from Johnson County where a mobile home was shifted off a foundation and multiple tree limbs were blown down. Effective bulk shear was 40 kts mid-afternoon, falling to 30 kts in Southern Illinois, while SRH dropped from 300 to 100 m2/s2 over the course of the afternoon. MLCAPE was notably low over Southern Illinois, only 500 J/kg near the Ohio River.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm downdraft winds blew down multiple tree limbs and caused a mobile home to slide off the foundation with areas of roofing damage.
